[Deputies of the governor general](/wiki/Deputy_of_the_Governor_General_of_Canada "Deputy of the Governor General of Canada") * [Secretary to the governor general](/wiki/Secretary_to_the_Governor_General_of_Canada "Secretary to the Governor General of Canada") * [Justices of the Supreme Court](/wiki/List_of_Justices_of_the_Supreme_Court_of_Canada "List of Justices of the Supreme Court of Canada")

([French](/wiki/French_language "French language"): *gouverneure générale du Canada*)[[n 1]](#cite_note-1) is the federal representative of the [Canadian monarch](/wiki/Monarchy_of_Canada "Monarchy of Canada"), currently King [Charles III](/wiki/Charles_III "Charles III"). The monarch of Canada is also sovereign and head of state of 14 other [Commonwealth realms](/wiki/Commonwealth_realm "Commonwealth realm") and resides in the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom"). The monarch, on the [advice](/wiki/Advice_(constitutional_law) "Advice (constitutional law)") of his or her [Canadian prime minister](/wiki/Canadian_prime_minister "Canadian prime minister"),[[1]](#cite_note-2) appoints a [governor general](/wiki/Governor_general "Governor general") to administer the [government of Canada](/wiki/Government_of_Canada "Government of Canada") in the monarch's name. The commission is for an indefinite period—known as serving *[at His Majesty's pleasure](/wiki/At_His_Majesty%27s_pleasure "At His Majesty's pleasure")*—usually five years. Since 1959, it has also been traditional to alternate between [francophone](/wiki/French_language_in_Canada "French language in Canada") and [anglophone](/wiki/English_language_in_Canada "English language in Canada") officeholders. The **governor general of Canada** ([French](/wiki/French_language "French language"): *gouverneure générale du Canada*)[[n 1]](#cite_note-1) is the federal representative of the [Canadian monarch](/wiki/Monarchy_of_Canada "Monarchy of Canada"), currently King [Charles III](/wiki/Charles_III "Charles III"). The monarch of Canada is also sovereign and head of state of 14 other [Commonwealth realms](/wiki/Commonwealth_realm "Commonwealth realm") and resides in the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom"). The monarch, on the [advice](/wiki/Advice_(constitutional_law) "Advice (constitutional law)") of his or her [Canadian prime minister](/wiki/Canadian_prime_minister "Canadian prime minister"),[[1]](#cite_note-2) appoints a [governor general](/wiki/Governor_general "Governor general") to administer the [government of Canada](/wiki/Government_of_Canada "Government of Canada") in the monarch's name. The commission is for an indefinite period—known as serving *[at His Majesty's pleasure](/wiki/At_His_Majesty%27s_pleasure "At His Majesty's pleasure")*—usually five years. Since 1959, it has also been traditional to alternate between [francophone](/wiki/French_language_in_Canada "French language in Canada") and [anglophone](/wiki/English_language_in_Canada "English language in Canada") officeholders. The 30th and current governor general is [Mary Simon](/wiki/Mary_Simon "Mary Simon"), who was sworn in on 26 July 2021. An [Inuk](/wiki/Inuk "Inuk") leader from [Nunavik](/wiki/Nunavik "Nunavik"), [Quebec](/wiki/Quebec "Quebec"), Simon is the first [Indigenous person](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples_in_Canada "Indigenous peoples in Canada") to hold the office.[[2]](#cite_note-3)

As the sovereign's representative, the governor general carries out the day-to-day constitutional and ceremonial duties of the monarch. The constitutional duties include appointing [lieutenant governors](/wiki/Lieutenant_governor_(Canada) "Lieutenant governor (Canada)"), [Supreme Court](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_Canada "Supreme Court of Canada") justices, and [senators](/wiki/Senate_of_Canada "Senate of Canada"); signing [orders-in-council](/wiki/Orders-in-council "Orders-in-council"); summoning, [proroguing](/wiki/Prorogation_in_Canada "Prorogation in Canada"), and dissolving the [federal parliament](/wiki/Parliament_of_Canada "Parliament of Canada"); granting [royal assent](/wiki/Royal_assent "Royal assent") to bills; calling elections; and signing commissions for officers of the [Canadian Armed Forces](/wiki/Canadian_Armed_Forces "Canadian Armed Forces"). The ceremonial duties include delivering the [speech from the throne](/wiki/Speech_from_the_throne#Commonwealth_realms "Speech from the throne") at the [state opening of parliament](/wiki/Opening_of_the_Canadian_parliament "Opening of the Canadian parliament"); accepting [letters of credence](/wiki/Letters_of_credence "Letters of credence") from incoming ambassadors; and distributing [honours, decorations, and medals](/wiki/Orders,_decorations,_and_medals_of_Canada "Orders, decorations, and medals of Canada"). Per the tenets of [responsible government](/wiki/Responsible_government "Responsible government"), the governor general acts almost always (except on the matter of honours) on the advice of the prime minister.

The office began in the 17th century, when the French Crown appointed governors of the [colony of Canada](/wiki/Canada_(New_France) "Canada (New France)") and, following the British conquest of the colony in 1763, the British monarch appointed governors of the [Province of Quebec](/wiki/Province_of_Quebec_(1763%E2%80%931791) "Province of Quebec (1763–1791)") (later [the Canadas](/wiki/The_Canadas "The Canadas")). Consequently, the office is, along with the Crown, the oldest continuous institution in Canada.[[3]](#cite_note-MacLeod34-4) The present version of the office emerged with [Canadian Confederation](/wiki/Canadian_Confederation "Canadian Confederation") and the passing of the *[British North America Act, 1867](/wiki/British_North_America_Act,_1867 "British North America Act, 1867")*.[[4]](#cite_note-5)

Although the post initially still represented the [government of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Government_of_the_United_Kingdom "Government of the United Kingdom") (that is, the monarch [in his British council](/wiki/King-in-Council "King-in-Council")), the office was gradually Canadianized until, with the passage of the *[Statute of Westminster, 1931](/wiki/Statute_of_Westminster,_1931 "Statute of Westminster, 1931")*, and the establishment of a separate and uniquely Canadian monarchy, the governor general became the direct personal representative of the independently Canadian sovereign (the monarch in his Canadian council).[[5]](#cite_note-6)[[6]](#cite_note-GGParl-7)[[7]](#cite_note-MacLeod35-8)[[8]](#cite_note-DCH3-9) Throughout this process of gradually increasing Canadian independence, the role of governor general took on additional responsibilities, such as acting as [commander-in-chief of the Canadian militia](/wiki/Commander-in-Chief_of_the_Canadian_Armed_Forces "Commander-in-Chief of the Canadian Armed Forces") in the monarch's stead,[[9]](#cite_note-GGCinC-10)[[10]](#cite_note-CA1867-15-11) and, in 1927, the first official international visit by a governor general was made.[[11]](#cite_note-Hubbard166-12)[[12]](#cite_note-13) In 1947, King [George VI](/wiki/George_VI "George VI") issued [letters patent](/wiki/Letters_Patent,_1947 "Letters Patent, 1947") allowing the [viceroy](/wiki/Viceroy "Viceroy") to exercise almost all powers on behalf of the monarch. As a matter of law, however, the governor general is not in the same constitutional position as the sovereign;[[13]](#cite_note-walters-14) the office itself does not, as such, possess any powers of the [royal prerogative](/wiki/Royal_prerogative "Royal prerogative"). Any constitutional amendment that affects [the Crown](/wiki/The_Crown "The Crown"), including the office of governor general, requires the unanimous consent of each provincial legislative assembly as well as the [Senate](/wiki/Senate_of_Canada "Senate of Canada") and [House of Commons of Canada](/wiki/House_of_Commons_of_Canada "House of Commons of Canada").

## Appointment

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Governor_General_of_Canada&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Appointment")]

[![Letters Patent 1947](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/09/Letters_Patent_1947_page1.png/250px-Letters_Patent_1947_page1.png)](/wiki/File:Letters_Patent_1947_page1.png)

First page of the proclamation of the *[Letters Patent, 1947](/wiki/Letters_Patent,_1947 "Letters Patent, 1947")*, as published in the [Canada Gazette](/wiki/Canada_Gazette "Canada Gazette")

The position of governor general is mandated by both the *[Constitution Act, 1867](/wiki/Constitution_Act,_1867 "Constitution Act, 1867")* (formerly known as the *British North America Act, 1867*), and the *[Letters Patent, 1947](/wiki/Letters_Patent,_1947 "Letters Patent, 1947")* by King [George VI](/wiki/George_VI "George VI").[[14]](#cite_note-GVII-15) As such, on the recommendation of the [Canadian prime minister](/wiki/Canadian_prime_minister "Canadian prime minister"), the Canadian monarch appoints the governor general by commission, prepared in Canada, and issued under the [Great Seal of Canada](/wiki/Great_Seal_of_Canada "Great Seal of Canada") and with the [royal sign-manual](/wiki/Royal_sign-manual "Royal sign-manual").[[15]](#cite_note-GoC105-16) (Until the appointment of [Vincent Massey](/wiki/Vincent_Massey "Vincent Massey") in 1952, the royal commission was authorized by the monarch's signature and [signet](/wiki/Seal_(emblem) "Seal (emblem)").[[15]](#cite_note-GoC105-16)) That individual is, from then until being sworn in, referred to as the *governor general-designate*.[[20]](#cite_note-21)

Besides the administration of the oaths of office, there is no set formula for the swearing-in of a governor general-designate.[[17]](#cite_note-DCHGGDes-18) Though there may therefore be variations to the following, the appointee will usually travel to [Ottawa](/wiki/Ottawa "Ottawa"), there receiving an official welcome and taking up residence at [7 Rideau Gate](/wiki/7_Rideau_Gate "7 Rideau Gate"),[[17]](#cite_note-DCHGGDes-18)[[21]](#cite_note-22) and will begin preparations for their upcoming role, meeting with various high-level officials to ensure a smooth transition between governors general. The sovereign will also hold an [audience](/wiki/Audience_(meeting) "Audience (meeting)") with the appointee and will at that time induct both the governor general-designate and their spouse into the [Order of Canada](/wiki/Order_of_Canada "Order of Canada") as Companions, as well as appointing the former as a Commander of both the [Order of Military Merit](/wiki/Order_of_Military_Merit_(Canada) "Order of Military Merit (Canada)") and the [Order of Merit of the Police Forces](/wiki/Order_of_Merit_of_the_Police_Forces "Order of Merit of the Police Forces") (should either person not have already received either of those honours).[[17]](#cite_note-DCHGGDes-18)

The incumbent will generally serve for at least five years; though, this is only a developed [convention](/wiki/Constitutional_convention_(political_custom) "Constitutional convention (political custom)") and has been criticized as too short for an office meant to represent a Crown and sovereign meant to embody continuity.[[22]](#cite_note-23) The governor general serves "[at His Majesty's pleasure](/wiki/At_His_Majesty%27s_pleasure "At His Majesty's pleasure")" (or the *royal pleasure*),[[23]](#cite_note-Gaz139-24) meaning the prime minister may recommend to the King that the viceroy remain in his service for a longer period of time; some governors general have been in office for more than seven years.[[n 2]](#cite_note-Term1-25) No additional formalities are necessary for such an "extension"; the governor general carries on until death,[[n 3]](#cite_note-Term3-26) resignation,[[n 4]](#cite_note-Term2-27) or the appointment of his or her successor.[[24]](#cite_note-GoC106-28) Only once, with the [Earl Alexander of Tunis](/wiki/Harold_Alexander,_1st_Earl_Alexander_of_Tunis "Harold Alexander, 1st Earl Alexander of Tunis"), has a royal proclamation been issued to end a governor general's incumbency.[[n 5]](#cite_note-Alex-29)

Should a governor general die, resign, or leave the country for longer than one month, the [chief justice of Canada](/wiki/Chief_justice_of_Canada "Chief justice of Canada") (or, if that position is vacant or unavailable, the senior puisne justice of the Supreme Court) serves as the [administrator of the government of Canada](/wiki/Administrator_of_the_Government_of_Canada "Administrator of the Government of Canada") and exercises all powers of the governor general.[[n 6]](#cite_note-Admin-30)

### Selection

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Governor_General_of_Canada&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Selection")]

In a speech on the subject of [Confederation](/wiki/Canadian_Confederation "Canadian Confederation"), made in 1866 to the [Legislative Assembly of the Province of Canada](/wiki/Legislative_Assembly_of_the_Province_of_Canada "Legislative Assembly of the Province of Canada"), [John A. Macdonald](/wiki/John_A._Macdonald "John A. Macdonald") said of the planned governor, "we place no restriction on Her Majesty's prerogative in the selection of her representative ... The sovereign has unrestricted freedom of choice ... We leave that to Her Majesty in all confidence."[[25]](#cite_note-31) However, between 1867 and 1931, governors general were appointed by the monarch on the advice of the [British Cabinet](/wiki/Cabinet_of_the_United_Kingdom "Cabinet of the United Kingdom"); until 1890, by the [secretary of state for the colonies](/wiki/Secretary_of_state_for_the_colonies "Secretary of state for the colonies") for approval by the prime minister. After that, a policy of consulting the Canadian Cabinet was established; though, this process was not always followed.[[26]](#cite_note-Lib-32)

The [Balfour Declaration of 1926](/wiki/Balfour_Declaration_of_1926 "Balfour Declaration of 1926") recognized the governor general as no longer a stand-in for the British government, but, rather a direct representative of the Crown. At the [Imperial Conference in 1930](/wiki/1930_Imperial_Conference "1930 Imperial Conference"), it was decided that the Commonwealth ministers would "tender their formal advice after informal consultation with His Majesty", thereby settling the current procedure wherein the monarch appoints the governor general on the advice of the Canadian prime minister only.[[26]](#cite_note-Lib-32) This was codified in the *[Statute of Westminster, 1931](/wiki/Statute_of_Westminster,_1931 "Statute of Westminster, 1931")*.

How many names the prime minister puts forward to the sovereign has varied. [Richard Bennett](/wiki/R._B._Bennett "R. B. Bennett") suggested a number of names in an informal letter to King [George V](/wiki/George_V "George V") in 1930, all of which the King approved, leading Bennett to pick [Vere Ponsonby, 9th Earl of Bessborough](/wiki/Vere_Ponsonby,_9th_Earl_of_Bessborough "Vere Ponsonby, 9th Earl of Bessborough"). [Mackenzie King](/wiki/Mackenzie_King "Mackenzie King") in 1945 gave three names to King George VI, who chose [Harold Alexander](/wiki/Harold_Alexander,_1st_Earl_Alexander_of_Tunis "Harold Alexander, 1st Earl Alexander of Tunis") (later the Viscount Alexander of Tunis). Mackenzie King then made the formal recommendation to the King, which was accepted. [Louis St. Laurent](/wiki/Louis_St._Laurent "Louis St. Laurent"), however, gave only one name—Massey's—to George VI.[[15]](#cite_note-GoC105-16) There is typically informal consultation between the prime minister and sovereign, either directly or via the incumbent governor general, on the appointment of the next viceroy before the prime minister gives his official advice to the monarch.[[15]](#cite_note-GoC105-16)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ad/RoyalVisitChateauLaurier.jpg/250px-RoyalVisitChateauLaurier.jpg)](/wiki/File:RoyalVisitChateauLaurier.jpg)

(Left to right) The wife of [Thomas Crerar](/wiki/Thomas_Crerar "Thomas Crerar"), King [George VI](/wiki/George_VI "George VI"), Prime Minister [William Lyon Mackenzie King](/wiki/William_Lyon_Mackenzie_King "William Lyon Mackenzie King"), [Queen Elizabeth](/wiki/Queen_Elizabeth_The_Queen_Mother "Queen Elizabeth The Queen Mother"), and Governor General the [Lord Tweedsmuir](/wiki/John_Buchan "John Buchan") at the [Château Laurier](/wiki/Ch%C3%A2teau_Laurier "Château Laurier") hotel in Ottawa, 1939. Mackenzie King was the only leader of the opposition to be involved in the appointment of a governor general, in 1935 deciding with then-prime minister [Richard Bennett](/wiki/R._B._Bennett "R. B. Bennett") to choose Tweedsmuir.

The only time the [leader of the opposition](/wiki/Leader_of_the_Official_Opposition_(Canada) "Leader of the Official Opposition (Canada)") was consulted on the choice of name to put forward to the monarch for appointment as viceroy was when [Lord Tweedsmuir](/wiki/John_Buchan "John Buchan")'s predecessor, Bessborough, wished to resign as governor general coincidentally just ahead of Parliament reaching the end of its maximum five year life in July 1935, after which an election was required. Mackenzie King, at the time leader of the opposition, expected to win a majority in the upcoming election and stated he would not accept a governor general recommended by then-Prime Minister Bennett, which was a constitutionally unjustifiable position for Mackenzie King to take. King George V became concerned and instructed Bessborough to either remain in office until after the election or bring Bennett and Mackenzie King together to agree on a nominee for his replacement. The governor general consulted with the prime minister and leader of the opposition and settled on Tweedsmuir.[[27]](#cite_note-33)

Until 1952, all governors general were members of the British aristocracy or former British military officers, diplomats, or politicians raised to the [peerage](/wiki/Peerage_of_the_United_Kingdom "Peerage of the United Kingdom"). These viceroys had spent no or little time in Canada prior to their appointment; though it was claimed as their travel schedules were so extensive that they could "learn more about Canada in five years than many Canadians in a lifetime".[[28]](#cite_note-34) The idea of a Canadian being appointed governor general was raised as early as 1919, when, at the [Paris Peace Conference](/wiki/Paris_Peace_Conference,_1919 "Paris Peace Conference, 1919"), Canadian prime minister Sir [Robert Borden](/wiki/Robert_Borden "Robert Borden"), consulted with South African prime minister [Louis Botha](/wiki/Louis_Botha "Louis Botha"), agreeing that viceregal appointees should be long-term residents of their respective [dominions](/wiki/Dominion "Dominion").[[29]](#cite_note-35) Calls for just such an individual to be made viceroy came again in the late 1930s,[[30]](#cite_note-36) but, it was not until Massey's appointment by King George VI in 1952 that the position was filled by a Canadian-born individual. The prime minister at the time, Louis St. Laurent, wrote in a letter to the media, "[i]t seems to me no one of the King's subjects, wherever he resides, should be considered unworthy to represent the King, provided he has the personal qualifications and a position in the community which are consonant with the dignity and responsibility of that office."[[26]](#cite_note-Lib-32) Massey stated of this, "a Canadian [as governor general] makes it far easier to look on the Crown as our own and on the Sovereign as Queen of Canada."[[31]](#cite_note-37)

This practice continued until 1999, when Queen [Elizabeth II](/wiki/Elizabeth_II "Elizabeth II") commissioned as her representative [Adrienne Clarkson](/wiki/Adrienne_Clarkson "Adrienne Clarkson"), a [Hong Kong](/wiki/Hong_Kong "Hong Kong")-born [refugee](/wiki/Refugee "Refugee") to Canada. Moreover, the practice of alternating between [francophone](/wiki/French_Canadian "French Canadian") and [anglophone](/wiki/English_Canadian "English Canadian") Canadians was instituted with the appointment of Vanier, a francophone who succeeded the anglophone Massey. All persons whose names are put forward to the King for approval must first undergo [background checks](/wiki/Background_check "Background check") by the [Royal Canadian Mounted Police](/wiki/Royal_Canadian_Mounted_Police "Royal Canadian Mounted Police") and the [Canadian Security Intelligence Service](/wiki/Canadian_Security_Intelligence_Service "Canadian Security Intelligence Service").[[32]](#cite_note-38)[[33]](#cite_note-CBCPremiers-39)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c9/Massey-moccasins.jpg/250px-Massey-moccasins.jpg)](/wiki/File:Massey-moccasins.jpg)

[Vincent Massey](/wiki/Vincent_Massey "Vincent Massey") (left), the first Canadian-born viceroy since [Confederation](/wiki/Canadian_Confederation "Canadian Confederation")

Although required by the tenets of [constitutional monarchy](/wiki/Constitutional_monarchy "Constitutional monarchy") to be [nonpartisan](/wiki/Nonpartisan_politician "Nonpartisan politician") while in office, governors general were often former politicians; a number held seats in the British [House of Lords](/wiki/House_of_Lords "House of Lords") by virtue of their inclusion in the peerage. Appointments of former ministers of the Crown in the 1980s and 1990s were criticized by [Peter H. Russell](/wiki/Peter_H._Russell "Peter H. Russell"), who stated in 2009: "much of [the] advantage of the monarchical system is lost in Canada when prime ministers recommend partisan colleagues to be appointed governor general and represent [the King]."[[34]](#cite_note-40) Clarkson was the first governor general in Canadian history with neither a political or military background, as well as the first [Asian-Canadian](/wiki/Asian-Canadian "Asian-Canadian") and the second woman, following on [Jeanne Sauvé](/wiki/Jeanne_Sauv%C3%A9 "Jeanne Sauvé"). The third woman to hold this position was also the first [Caribbean-Canadian](/wiki/Black_Canadians "Black Canadians") governor general, [Michaëlle Jean](/wiki/Micha%C3%ABlle_Jean "Michaëlle Jean").

There have been, from time to time, proposals put forward for modifications to the selection process. [Citizens for a Canadian Republic](/wiki/Citizens_for_a_Canadian_Republic "Citizens for a Canadian Republic") has advocated the election of the nominee to the sovereign, either by [popular](/wiki/Universal_suffrage "Universal suffrage") or parliamentary vote;[[35]](#cite_note-41) a proposal echoed by Clarkson, who called for the prime minister's choice to not only be vetted by a parliamentary committee,[[36]](#cite_note-42)[[37]](#cite_note-43) but, also submit to a televised quiz on [Canadiana](/wiki/Canadiana "Canadiana").[[38]](#cite_note-44) Constitutional scholars, editorial boards, and the [Monarchist League of Canada](/wiki/Monarchist_League_of_Canada "Monarchist League of Canada") have argued against any such constitutional tinkering with the viceregal appointment process, stating that the position being "not elected is an asset, not a handicap", and that an election would politicize the office, thereby undermining the impartiality necessary to the proper functioning of the governor general.[[39]](#cite_note-45)[[40]](#cite_note-46) Retired [University of Ottawa](/wiki/University_of_Ottawa "University of Ottawa") professor John E. Trent proposed the governor general be head of state and selected by the Officers of the [Order of Canada](/wiki/Order_of_Canada "Order of Canada"),[[41]](#cite_note-47) something Chris Selley argued would politicize both the head of state and the Order of Canada, itself.[[42]](#cite_note-48) In 2021, [Grand Chief](/wiki/Treaty_6#Grand_chiefs_of_the_Confederacy "Treaty 6") Vernon Watchmaker and Chief Germaine Anderson of the [Six Nations of the Grand River](/wiki/Six_Nations_of_the_Grand_River "Six Nations of the Grand River") wrote to Queen Elizabeth II, suggesting that the federal Cabinet consult [treaty nations](/wiki/First_Nations_in_Canada "First Nations in Canada") before the appointment of a new governor general, stating, "we are [partners with the Crown](/wiki/Monarchy_of_Canada_and_the_Indigenous_peoples_of_Canada "Monarchy of Canada and the Indigenous peoples of Canada")."[[43]](#cite_note-49)

A new approach was used in 2010 for the selection of [David Johnston](/wiki/David_Johnston_(governor_general) "David Johnston (governor general)") as governor general-designate. For the task, prime minister [Stephen Harper](/wiki/Stephen_Harper "Stephen Harper") convened a special search group—the Governor General Consultation Committee[[44]](#cite_note-50)—which was instructed to find a non-partisan candidate who would respect the monarchical aspects of the viceregal office and conducted extensive consultations with more than 200 people across the country.[[49]](#cite_note-55) In 2012, the committee was made permanent and renamed as the [Advisory Committee on Vice-Regal Appointments](/wiki/Advisory_Committee_on_Vice-Regal_Appointments "Advisory Committee on Vice-Regal Appointments"), with a modified membership and its scope broadened to include the appointment of provincial [lieutenant governors](/wiki/Lieutenant_governor_(Canada) "Lieutenant governor (Canada)") and territorial [commissioners](/wiki/Commissioner#Canadian_territories "Commissioner") (though, the latter are not personal representatives of the monarch).[[50]](#cite_note-56) However, the next ministry, headed by [Justin Trudeau](/wiki/Justin_Trudeau "Justin Trudeau"), disbanded the committee in 2017,[[51]](#cite_note-57) before he recommended Payette as Johnston's successor that year.[[52]](#cite_note-58) Following Payette's resignation, Trudeau formed the Advisory Group on the Selection of the Next Governor General,[[26]](#cite_note-Lib-32) which selected [Mary Simon](/wiki/Mary_Simon "Mary Simon") for appointment as vicereine.[[53]](#cite_note-59)

### Swearing-in ceremony

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Governor_General_of_Canada&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Swearing-in ceremony")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/7/7d/Jean-Oath.jpg/250px-Jean-Oath.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jean-Oath.jpg)

[Michaëlle Jean](/wiki/Micha%C3%ABlle_Jean "Michaëlle Jean") swearing the oaths of office as administered by puisne justice [Michel Bastarache](/wiki/Michel_Bastarache "Michel Bastarache"), 27 September 2005

The swearing-in ceremony begins with the arrival at [7 Rideau Gate](/wiki/7_Rideau_Gate "7 Rideau Gate") of one of the [ministers of the Crown](/wiki/Ministers_of_the_Crown "Ministers of the Crown"), who then accompanies the governor general-designate to [Parliament Hill](/wiki/Parliament_Hill "Parliament Hill"), where a [Canadian Forces](/wiki/Canadian_Forces "Canadian Forces") [guard of honour](/wiki/Guard_of_honour "Guard of honour") (consisting of the [Army](/wiki/Canadian_Army "Canadian Army") Guard, [Royal Canadian Air Force](/wiki/Royal_Canadian_Air_Force "Royal Canadian Air Force") Guard, and Flag Party of the [Royal Canadian Navy](/wiki/Royal_Canadian_Navy "Royal Canadian Navy")) awaits to give a [general salute](/wiki/General_salute "General salute"). From there, the party is led by the King's parliamentary messenger—the [usher of the Black Rod](/wiki/Usher_of_the_Black_Rod_of_the_Senate_of_Canada "Usher of the Black Rod of the Senate of Canada")—to the [Senate chamber](/wiki/Senate_of_Canada "Senate of Canada"), wherein all [justices of the Supreme Court](/wiki/List_of_Justices_of_the_Supreme_Court_of_Canada "List of Justices of the Supreme Court of Canada"), senators, [members of parliament](/wiki/Member_of_Parliament_(Canada) "Member of Parliament (Canada)"), and other guests are assembled. The King's commission for the governor general-designate is then read aloud by the [secretary to the governor general](/wiki/Secretary_to_the_Governor_General_of_Canada "Secretary to the Governor General of Canada") and the required oaths are administered to the appointee by either the [chief justice](/wiki/Chief_Justice_of_Canada "Chief Justice of Canada") or one of the [puisne justices](/wiki/List_of_Puisne_Justices_of_the_Supreme_Court_of_Canada "List of Puisne Justices of the Supreme Court of Canada") of the [Supreme Court](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_Canada "Supreme Court of Canada"). The three oaths are the [Oath of Allegiance](/wiki/Oath_of_Allegiance_(Canada) "Oath of Allegiance (Canada)"), the Oath of Office as Governor General and Commander-in-Chief, and the Oath as Keeper of the [Great Seal of Canada](/wiki/Great_Seal_of_Canada "Great Seal of Canada"). With the affixing of their signature to these three solemn promises, the individual is officially the governor general, and at that moment the [flag of the governor general of Canada](/wiki/Flag_of_the_governor_general_of_Canada "Flag of the governor general of Canada") is raised on the [Peace Tower](/wiki/Peace_Tower "Peace Tower"),[[17]](#cite_note-DCHGGDes-18) the "[Vice Regal Salute](/wiki/Vice_Regal_Salute "Vice Regal Salute")" is played by the [Central Band of the Canadian Armed Forces](/wiki/Central_Band_of_the_Canadian_Armed_Forces "Central Band of the Canadian Armed Forces"), and a [21-gun salute](/wiki/21-gun_salute "21-gun salute") is conducted by the [Royal Regiment of Canadian Artillery](/wiki/Royal_Regiment_of_Canadian_Artillery "Royal Regiment of Canadian Artillery"). The governor general is seated on the throne while a prayer is read, and then receives the Great Seal of Canada (which is passed to the [registrar general](/wiki/Registrar_General_of_Canada "Registrar General of Canada") for protection),[[54]](#cite_note-60) as well as the chains of both the chancellor of the Order of Canada and of the [Order of Military Merit](/wiki/Order_of_Military_Merit_(Canada) "Order of Military Merit (Canada)").[[55]](#cite_note-61) The governor general then gives a speech, outlining causes they will champion during their time as viceroy.[[17]](#cite_note-DCHGGDes-18)

## Role

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Governor_General_of_Canada&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Role")]

Further information: [Monarchy of Canada § International and domestic aspects](/wiki/Monarchy_of_Canada#International_and_domestic_aspects "Monarchy of Canada")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9f/Vladimir_Putin_in_Canada_18-19_December_2000-2.jpg/250px-Vladimir_Putin_in_Canada_18-19_December_2000-2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Vladimir_Putin_in_Canada_18-19_December_2000-2.jpg)

Governor General [Adrienne Clarkson](/wiki/Adrienne_Clarkson "Adrienne Clarkson") (right) meets with [Russian president](/wiki/President_of_Russia "President of Russia") [Vladimir Putin](/wiki/Vladimir_Putin "Vladimir Putin") (left) in the governor general's study of [Rideau Hall](/wiki/Rideau_Hall "Rideau Hall"), 18 December 2000

> If, and because your Governor-General is in the service of the Crown, he is, therefore ... in the service of Canada ... [A]loof though he be from actual executive responsibility, his attitude must be that of ceaseless and watchful readiness to take part ... in the fostering of every influence that will sweeten and elevate public life; to ... join in making known the resources and developments of the country; to vindicate, if required, the rights of the people and the ordinariness and Constitution, and lastly, to promote by all means in his power, without reference to class or creed, every movement and every institution calculated to forward the social, moral, and religious welfare of the inhabitants of the Dominion.[[56]](#cite_note-62)

Governor General [the Marquess of Aberdeen](/wiki/John_Hamilton-Gordon,_1st_Marquess_of_Aberdeen_and_Temair "John Hamilton-Gordon, 1st Marquess of Aberdeen and Temair"), 1893

Canada [shares the person of the sovereign](/wiki/Personal_union "Personal union") equally with [14 other countries](/wiki/Commonwealth_realm "Commonwealth realm") in the [Commonwealth of Nations](/wiki/Commonwealth_of_Nations "Commonwealth of Nations") and that individual, in the monarch's capacity as the Canadian sovereign, has [10 other legal personas](/wiki/Monarchy_of_Canada#Federal_and_provincial_aspects "Monarchy of Canada") within the [Canadian federation](/wiki/Canadian_federalism "Canadian federalism"). As the sovereign works and resides in the United Kingdom, the governor general's primary task is to perform federal constitutional duties on behalf of the monarch.[[57]](#cite_note-63)[[58]](#cite_note-64) As such, the governor general carries on "the government of Canada on behalf and in the name of the sovereign".[[59]](#cite_note-65)

The governor general acts within the principles of [parliamentary democracy](/wiki/Parliamentary_democracy "Parliamentary democracy") and [responsible government](/wiki/Responsible_government "Responsible government") as a guarantor of continuous and stable governance and as a [nonpartisan](/wiki/Nonpartisanism "Nonpartisanism") safeguard against the abuse of power.[[60]](#cite_note-66)[[61]](#cite_note-GGDuties-67)[[62]](#cite_note-68) For the most part, however, the powers of the Crown are exercised on a day-to-day basis by elected and appointed individuals, leaving the governor general to perform the various ceremonial duties the sovereign otherwise carries out when in the country; at such a moment, the governor general removes him or herself from public,[[n 7]](#cite_note-GGMon-70) though the presence of the monarch does not affect the governor general's ability to perform governmental roles.[[64]](#cite_note-71)[[65]](#cite_note-Heard-72)

Past governor general [John Campbell, Marquess of Lorne](/wiki/John_Campbell,_9th_Duke_of_Argyll "John Campbell, 9th Duke of Argyll"), said of the job, "it is no easy thing to be a governor general of Canada. You must have the patience of a saint, the smile of a [cherub](/wiki/Cherub "Cherub"), the generosity of an Indian prince, and the back of a camel",[[66]](#cite_note-73) and the [Earl of Dufferin](/wiki/Frederick_Hamilton-Temple-Blackwood,_1st_Marquess_of_Dufferin_and_Ava "Frederick Hamilton-Temple-Blackwood, 1st Marquess of Dufferin and Ava") stated that the governor general is "a representative of all that is august, stable, and sedate in the government, the history and the traditions of the country; incapable of partizanship and lifted far above the atmosphere of faction; without adherents to reward or opponents to oust from office; docile to the suggestions of his Ministers and, yet, securing to the people the certainty of being able to get rid of an administration or parliament the moment either had forfeited their confidence."[[67]](#cite_note-74)

### Constitutional role

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Governor_General_of_Canada&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Constitutional role")]

Further information: [Monarchy of Canada § Federal constitutional role](/wiki/Monarchy_of_Canada#Federal_constitutional_role "Monarchy of Canada")

All [executive](/wiki/Executive_(government) "Executive (government)"), [legislative](/wiki/Legislative "Legislative"), and [judicial](/wiki/Judicial "Judicial") power in and over Canada is vested in the monarch.[[68]](#cite_note-75)[[69]](#cite_note-MacLeod17-76) The governor general is permitted to exercise most of this power, including the [royal prerogative](/wiki/Royal_prerogative "Royal prerogative"), in the sovereign's name; some as outlined in the *Constitution Act, 1867*, and some through various [letters patent](/wiki/Letters_patent "Letters patent") issued over the decades, particularly [those from 1947](/wiki/Letters_Patent,_1947 "Letters Patent, 1947") that constitute the Office of Governor General of Canada.[[70]](#cite_note-77) The 1947 letters patent state, "and We do hereby authorize and empower Our Governor General, with the advice of [Our Privy Council for Canada](/wiki/King%27s_Privy_Council_for_Canada "King's Privy Council for Canada") or of any members thereof or individually, as the case requires, to exercise all powers and authorities lawfully belonging to Us in respect of Canada."[[71]](#cite_note-78) The office itself does not, however, independently possess any powers of the royal prerogative, only exercising the Crown's powers with its permission; a fact the *Constitution Act, 1867*, left unchanged.[[72]](#cite_note-79) Among other duties, the monarch retains the sole right to appoint the governor general.[[7]](#cite_note-MacLeod35-8) It is also stipulated that the governor general [may appoint deputies](/wiki/Deputy_of_the_Governor_General_of_Canada "Deputy of the Governor General of Canada")—usually Supreme Court justices and the secretary to the governor general—who can perform some of the viceroy's constitutional duties in the governor general's absence,[[73]](#cite_note-80) and the chief justice of the Supreme Court (or a puisne justice in the chief justice's absence) will act as the administrator of the government upon the death or removal, as well as the incapacitation, or absence of the governor general for more than one month.[[74]](#cite_note-81)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/44/Swearing_In_-_Assermentation_%2836647507344%29.jpg/250px-Swearing_In_-_Assermentation_%2836647507344%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Swearing_In_-_Assermentation_(36647507344).jpg)

[Andrew Scheer](/wiki/Andrew_Scheer "Andrew Scheer") (right), then [Leader of Her Majesty's Loyal Opposition](/wiki/Leader_of_the_Official_Opposition_(Canada) "Leader of the Official Opposition (Canada)"), being admitted to the [Queen's Privy Council for Canada](/wiki/Queen%27s_Privy_Council_for_Canada "Queen's Privy Council for Canada") by Governor General [David Johnston](/wiki/David_Johnston_(governor_general) "David Johnston (governor general)") (centre) at [Rideau Hall](/wiki/Rideau_Hall "Rideau Hall"), 25 September 2017

The governor general is required by the *Constitution Act, 1867*, to appoint for life persons to the [King's Privy Council for Canada](/wiki/King%27s_Privy_Council_for_Canada "King's Privy Council for Canada"),[[75]](#cite_note-82) who are all technically tasked with tendering to the monarch and viceroy guidance on the exercise of the royal prerogative. [Convention](/wiki/Convention_(norm)#Government "Convention (norm)") dictates, though, that the governor general must draw from the Privy Council an individual to appoint as prime minister. In almost all cases, this is the member of Parliament who commands the [confidence](/wiki/Confidence_and_supply "Confidence and supply") of the [House of Commons](/wiki/House_of_Commons_of_Canada "House of Commons of Canada"), whom the governor general must appoint to the Privy Council, if that person is not already a member, so the individual can be appointed prime minister. The prime minister then advises the governor general to appoint other members of parliament to a committee of the Privy Council known as the [Cabinet](/wiki/Cabinet_of_Canada "Cabinet of Canada") and it is, in practice, only from this group of [ministers of the Crown](/wiki/Ministers_of_the_Crown "Ministers of the Crown") that the king and governor general will take [advice](/wiki/Advice_(constitutional_law) "Advice (constitutional law)") on the use of executive power;[[76]](#cite_note-83) an arrangement called the *[king-in-Council](/wiki/King-in-Council "King-in-Council")* or,[[69]](#cite_note-MacLeod17-76) more specifically, the *governor-in-Council*. In this capacity, the governor general will issue royal [proclamations](/wiki/Proclamation "Proclamation") and sign [orders in council](/wiki/Orders_in_council "Orders in council"). The governor-in-Council is also specifically tasked by the *Constitution Act, 1867*, to appoint in the monarch's name, the [lieutenant governors of the provinces](/wiki/Lieutenant_Governor_(Canada) "Lieutenant Governor (Canada)"),[[77]](#cite_note-84) [senators](/wiki/Senate_of_Canada "Senate of Canada"),[[78]](#cite_note-85) the [speaker of the Senate](/wiki/Speaker_of_the_Senate_of_Canada "Speaker of the Senate of Canada"),[[79]](#cite_note-86) [superior](/wiki/Superior_court "Superior court"), district, and county court judges in each province, except those of the Courts of Probate in [Nova Scotia](/wiki/Nova_Scotia "Nova Scotia") and [New Brunswick](/wiki/New_Brunswick "New Brunswick"),[[80]](#cite_note-87) and [high commissioners](/wiki/High_commissioner_(Commonwealth) "High commissioner (Commonwealth)") and ambassadors.[[81]](#cite_note-GoC152-88) The advice given by the Cabinet is, in order to ensure the stability of government, by political convention typically binding. The governor general has mainly only the right to advise, encourage, and warn; to offer valued counsel to the prime minister.[[82]](#cite_note-89)[[83]](#cite_note-90)

Both the King and his viceroy, however, may in exceptional circumstances invoke the [reserve powers](/wiki/Reserve_powers "Reserve powers"), which remain the Crown's final check against a ministry's abuse of power.[[n 8]](#cite_note-RP-91)[[84]](#cite_note-Commons-92) The reserve power of dismissal has never been used in Canada, although other reserve powers have been employed to force the prime minister to resign on two occasions. In 1896, prime minister [Charles Tupper](/wiki/Charles_Tupper "Charles Tupper") refused to step down after his party failed to win a majority in the House of Commons during [that year's election](/wiki/1896_Canadian_federal_election "1896 Canadian federal election"), leading governor general [Gordon](/wiki/John_Hamilton-Gordon,_1st_Marquess_of_Aberdeen_and_Temair "John Hamilton-Gordon, 1st Marquess of Aberdeen and Temair") to no longer recognize Tupper as prime minister and disapprove of several appointments Tupper had recommended. In 1925, the [King–Byng affair](/wiki/King%E2%80%93Byng_affair "King–Byng affair") took place, in which prime minister Mackenzie King, facing a [non-confidence motion](/wiki/Motion_of_no_confidence "Motion of no confidence") in the House of Commons, advised governor general Byng to [dissolve](/wiki/Dissolution_of_parliament "Dissolution of parliament") the new parliament, but Byng refused.

[Peter Hogg](/wiki/Peter_Hogg "Peter Hogg"), a constitutional scholar, has opined that "a system of responsible government cannot work without a formal head of state who is possessed of certain reserve powers." Further, [Eugene Forsey](/wiki/Eugene_Forsey "Eugene Forsey") stated "the reserve power is indeed, under our Constitution, an absolutely essential safeguard of democracy. It takes the place of the legal and judicial safeguards provided in the United States by written Constitutions, enforceable in the courts."

Within the dominions, until the 1920s, most reserve powers were exercised by a governor general on the advice of either the local or the British Cabinet, with the latter taking precedence. After the [Imperial Conference of 1926](/wiki/1926_Imperial_Conference "1926 Imperial Conference") produced the [Balfour Declaration](/wiki/Balfour_Declaration_of_1926 "Balfour Declaration of 1926"), formally establishing the autonomy and equal status of Commonwealth governments, governors general ceased to be advised in any way by British ministers.

It was decided at the same Imperial Conference that the governor general "should be kept as fully informed as His Majesty the King in Great Britain of Cabinet business and public affairs."[[85]](#cite_note-93) How frequently governors general and their prime ministers conversed has varied;[[81]](#cite_note-GoC152-88) some prime ministers have valued their meetings with the viceroy at the time.[[n 9]](#cite_note-95) However, the practice is usually informal[[81]](#cite_note-GoC152-88) and the prime minister will typically schedule a telephone call to request the governor general perform a significant task.[[88]](#cite_note-97)[[87]](#cite_note-Hopper-96) The governor general regularly receives the [minutes](/wiki/Minutes "Minutes") from cabinet meetings and any documents referred to in those minutes.[[89]](#cite_note-98)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fa/Tweedsmuir_speech.jpg/250px-Tweedsmuir_speech.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tweedsmuir_speech.jpg)

[Lord Tweedsmuir](/wiki/John_Buchan "John Buchan") gives the [Throne Speech](/wiki/Speech_from_the_throne "Speech from the throne") at the opening of the third session of the [18th Canadian Parliament](/wiki/18th_Canadian_Parliament "18th Canadian Parliament"), 27 January 1938

The governor general also summons parliament, reads the [speech from the throne](/wiki/Speech_from_the_throne "Speech from the throne"), and [prorogues](/wiki/Prorogation_in_Canada "Prorogation in Canada") and [dissolves](/wiki/Dissolution_of_parliament "Dissolution of parliament") parliament. The governor general grants [royal assent](/wiki/Royal_assent "Royal assent") in the king's name; legally, the governor general has three options: grant royal assent (making the bill a law), withhold royal assent (vetoing the bill), or reserve the bill for the signification of the king's pleasure (allowing the sovereign to personally grant or withhold assent).[[90]](#cite_note-99) If the governor general withholds the king's assent, the sovereign may within two years [disallow](/wiki/Disallowance_and_reservation_in_Canada "Disallowance and reservation in Canada") the bill, thereby annulling the law in question. No governor general has denied royal assent to a bill. Provincial viceroys, however, are able to reserve royal assent to provincial bills for the governor general, which was last invoked in 1961 by the [lieutenant governor of Saskatchewan](/wiki/Lieutenant_Governor_of_Saskatchewan "Lieutenant Governor of Saskatchewan").[[91]](#cite_note-100)

### Ceremonial role

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Governor_General_of_Canada&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Ceremonial role")]

Further information: [Monarchy of Canada § Cultural role](/wiki/Monarchy_of_Canada#Cultural_role "Monarchy of Canada")

With most constitutional functions lent to Cabinet, the governor general acts in a primarily ceremonial fashion. The governor general will host members of [Canada's royal family](/wiki/Canada%27s_royal_family "Canada's royal family"), as well as foreign royalty and heads of state, and will represent the King and country abroad on [state visits to other nations](/wiki/List_of_state_and_official_visits_by_Canada "List of state and official visits by Canada"),[[92]](#cite_note-GGrole-101)[[84]](#cite_note-Commons-92) though the monarch's permission is necessary, via the prime minister, for the viceroy to leave Canada.[[93]](#cite_note-102) Also as part of international relations, the governor general issues [letters of credence and of recall](/wiki/Letter_of_credence "Letter of credence") for Canadian [ambassadors](/wiki/Ambassador "Ambassador") and [high commissioners](/wiki/High_commissioner_(Commonwealth) "High commissioner (Commonwealth)") and receives the same from foreign ambassadors and other Commonwealth countries' high commissioners appointed to Canada.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3b/The_Governor_General_David_Johnston_greets_more_people_on_Canada_Day_%2828014776516%29.jpg/250px-The_Governor_General_David_Johnston_greets_more_people_on_Canada_Day_%2828014776516%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Governor_General_David_Johnston_greets_more_people_on_Canada_Day_(28014776516).jpg)

Governor general David Johnston greeting a crowd during Canada Day celebrations in Ottawa, July 2016

The governor general is also tasked with fostering national unity and pride.[[94]](#cite_note-Boyce-103) Queen Elizabeth II stated in 1959 to governor general Massey, "maintain[ing] the right relationship between the Crown and the people of Canada [is] the most important function among the many duties of the appointment which you have held with such distinction."[[95]](#cite_note-104) One way in which this is carried out is travelling the country and meeting with Canadians from [all regions](/wiki/List_of_regions_of_Canada "List of regions of Canada") and [ethnic groups in Canada](/wiki/Ethnic_groups_in_Canada "Ethnic groups in Canada"),[[92]](#cite_note-GGrole-101) continuing the tradition begun in 1869 by governor general [John Young](/wiki/John_Young,_1st_Baron_Lisgar "John Young, 1st Baron Lisgar").[[96]](#cite_note-105) The governor general will also induct individuals into the various [national orders](/wiki/Orders,_decorations,_and_medals_of_Canada "Orders, decorations, and medals of Canada") and present national medals and decorations. Similarly, the viceroy administers and distributes the [Governor General's Awards](/wiki/Governor_General%27s_Awards "Governor General's Awards"), and will also give out [awards associated with private organizations](/wiki/List_of_awards_presented_by_the_Governor_General_of_Canada "List of awards presented by the Governor General of Canada"), some of which are named for past governors general.[[92]](#cite_note-GGrole-101) During a federal election, the governor general will curtail these public duties, so as not to appear as though they are involving themselves in political affairs.

Although the constitution of Canada states that the "Command-in-Chief of the Land and Naval Militia, and of all Naval and Military Forces, of and in Canada, is hereby declared to continue and be vested in the Queen,"[[10]](#cite_note-CA1867-15-11) the governor general acts in his place as [commander-in-chief of the Canadian Forces](/wiki/Commander-in-Chief_of_the_Canadian_Forces "Commander-in-Chief of the Canadian Forces") and is permitted through the 1947 letters patent to use the title *Commander-in-Chief in and over Canada*.[[9]](#cite_note-GGCinC-10)[[14]](#cite_note-GVII-15) The position technically involves issuing commands for Canadian troops, airmen, and sailors, but is predominantly a ceremonial role in which the viceroy will visit [Canadian Forces bases](/wiki/Canadian_Forces_base "Canadian Forces base") across Canada and abroad to take part in military ceremonies, see troops off to and return from active duty, and encourage excellence and morale amongst the forces.[[9]](#cite_note-GGCinC-10) The governor general also serves as honorary [Colonel](/wiki/Colonel "Colonel") of three [household regiments](/wiki/Household_Division "Household Division"): the [Governor General's Horse Guards](/wiki/Governor_General%27s_Horse_Guards "Governor General's Horse Guards"), [Governor General's Foot Guards](/wiki/Governor_General%27s_Foot_Guards "Governor General's Foot Guards") and [Canadian Grenadier Guards](/wiki/Canadian_Grenadier_Guards "Canadian Grenadier Guards"). This ceremonial position is directly under that of [Colonel-in-Chief](/wiki/Colonel-in-Chief "Colonel-in-Chief"), which is held by the King. Since 1910, the governor general was also always made the chief scout for Canada, which was renamed *Chief Scout of Canada* after 1946 and again in 2011 as *[Patron Scout](/wiki/Scouts_Canada#Organizational_structure "Scouts Canada")*.[[97]](#cite_note-106)

## Residences and household

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Governor_General_of_Canada&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Residences and household")]

[Rideau Hall](/wiki/Rideau_Hall "Rideau Hall"), located in Ottawa, is the official residence of the Canadian monarch[[98]](#cite_note-107)[[99]](#cite_note-Toffoli-108) and of the governor general and is thus the location of the viceregal household and the Chancellery of Honours. For a part of each year since 1872, governors general have also resided at [the Citadel](/wiki/Citadelle_of_Quebec "Citadelle of Quebec") (*La Citadelle*) in [Quebec City, Quebec](/wiki/Quebec_City,_Quebec "Quebec City, Quebec").[[100]](#cite_note-109) A [governor general's wife](/wiki/Viceregal_consort_of_Canada "Viceregal consort of Canada") is known as the *chatelaine* of Rideau Hall, though there is no equivalent term for a governor general's husband.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b1/10_Rideau_Hall_P1350151.jpg/250px-10_Rideau_Hall_P1350151.jpg)](/wiki/File:10_Rideau_Hall_P1350151.jpg)

[Rideau Hall](/wiki/Rideau_Hall "Rideau Hall"), the primary residence of the governor general

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e9/Residence_of_the_Governor_General_%28Quebec%29_01.jpg/250px-Residence_of_the_Governor_General_%28Quebec%29_01.jpg)](/wiki/File:Residence_of_the_Governor_General_(Quebec)_01.jpg)

Secondary residence at the [Citadelle of Quebec](/wiki/Citadelle_of_Quebec "Citadelle of Quebec")

The viceregal household aids the governor general in the execution of the royal constitutional and ceremonial duties and is managed by the [secretary to the governor general](/wiki/Secretary_to_the_Governor_General_of_Canada "Secretary to the Governor General of Canada").[[101]](#cite_note-OSTGG-110) The Chancellery of Honours depends from the King and is thus also located at Rideau Hall and administered by the governor general. As such, the viceroy's secretary *ex officio* holds the position of [Herald Chancellor of Canada](/wiki/Herald_Chancellor_of_Canada "Herald Chancellor of Canada"),[[102]](#cite_note-Herald-111) overseeing the [Canadian Heraldic Authority](/wiki/Canadian_Heraldic_Authority "Canadian Heraldic Authority")—the mechanism of the Canadian honours system by which [armorial bearings](/wiki/Coat_of_arms "Coat of arms") are granted to Canadians by the governor general in the name of the sovereign.[[102]](#cite_note-Herald-111) These organized offices and support systems include [aides-de-camp](/wiki/Aide-de-camp#Canada "Aide-de-camp"), [press officers](/wiki/News_media "News media"), financial managers,[[101]](#cite_note-OSTGG-110) speech writers, trip organizers, event planners, protocol officers, chefs and other kitchen employees, waiters, and various cleaning staff, as well as visitors' centre staff and tour guides at both official residences. In this official and bureaucratic capacity, the entire household is often referred to as *[Government House](/wiki/Government_Houses_in_Canada "Government Houses in Canada")*[[103]](#cite_note-Gaz143-112) and its departments are funded through the normal [federal budgetary process](/wiki/Canadian_federal_budget "Canadian federal budget"),[[104]](#cite_note-113) as is the governor general's salary of [CAD$](/wiki/Canadian_dollar "Canadian dollar")288,900,[[105]](#cite_note-114) which has been taxed since 2013.[[106]](#cite_note-115)[[107]](#cite_note-116) Additional costs are incurred from separate ministries and organizations such as the [National Capital Commission](/wiki/National_Capital_Commission "National Capital Commission"), the [Department of National Defence](/wiki/Department_of_National_Defence_(Canada) "Department of National Defence (Canada)"), and the Royal Canadian Mounted Police.[[108]](#cite_note-117)

[The governor general's air transportation](/wiki/Royal_Canadian_Air_Force_VIP_aircraft "Royal Canadian Air Force VIP aircraft") is assigned to [412 Transport Squadron](/wiki/412_Transport_Squadron "412 Transport Squadron") of the Royal Canadian Air Force. The squadron uses [Bombardier Challenger 600](/wiki/Bombardier_Challenger_600 "Bombardier Challenger 600") VIP jets to transport the governor general to locations within and outside of Canada.

## Symbols and protocol

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Governor_General_of_Canada&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Symbols and protocol")]

As the personal representative of the monarch, the governor general follows only the sovereign in the [Canadian order of precedence](/wiki/Canadian_order_of_precedence "Canadian order of precedence"), preceding even other members of the royal family. Though the federal viceroy is considered *[primus inter pares](/wiki/Primus_inter_pares "Primus inter pares")* amongst provincial counterparts, the governor general also outranks the lieutenant governors in the federal sphere; at provincial functions, however, the relevant lieutenant governor, as the King's representative in the province, precedes the governor general.[[109]](#cite_note-118) The incumbent governor general and their spouse are also the only people in Canada, other than serving [Canadian ambassadors and high commissioners](/wiki/Ambassadors_and_High_Commissioners_from_Canada "Ambassadors and High Commissioners from Canada"), entitled to the use of the [style](/wiki/Style_(manner_of_address) "Style (manner of address)") *His* or *Her [Excellency](/wiki/Excellency "Excellency")* and the governor general is granted the additional honorific of *[the Right Honourable](/wiki/The_Right_Honourable "The Right Honourable")* for their time in office and for life afterwards.[[110]](#cite_note-DND11-2-119)[[111]](#cite_note-MacLeod37-120)[[112]](#cite_note-PCH_Titles-121)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/71/Micha%C3%ABlle_Jean_11-11-2007.jpg/250px-Micha%C3%ABlle_Jean_11-11-2007.jpg)](/wiki/File:Micha%C3%ABlle_Jean_11-11-2007.jpg)

[Michaëlle Jean](/wiki/Micha%C3%ABlle_Jean "Michaëlle Jean") wearing the insignia of the [Order of Canada](/wiki/Order_of_Canada "Order of Canada") and [Order of Military Merit](/wiki/Order_of_Military_Merit_(Canada) "Order of Military Merit (Canada)") along with the [Canadian Forces' Decoration](/wiki/Canadian_Forces%27_Decoration "Canadian Forces' Decoration")

Until 1952, all governors general of Canada were members of the [peerage](/wiki/Peerage "Peerage") or heir apparent to a peerage. Typically, individuals appointed as federal viceroy were already a peer, either by inheriting the title, such as the [Duke of Devonshire](/wiki/Victor_Cavendish,_9th_Duke_of_Devonshire "Victor Cavendish, 9th Duke of Devonshire"), or by prior elevation by the sovereign in their own right, as was the case with [Earl Alexander of Tunis](/wiki/Harold_Alexander,_1st_Earl_Alexander_of_Tunis "Harold Alexander, 1st Earl Alexander of Tunis"). None were [life peers](/wiki/Life_peer "Life peer"), the [Life Peerages Act 1958](/wiki/Life_Peerages_Act_1958 "Life Peerages Act 1958") postdating the beginning of the tradition of appointing Canadian citizens as governor general. John Buchan was, in preparation for his appointment as governor general, made the Baron Tweedsmuir of Elsfield in the County of Oxford by King [George V](/wiki/George_V "George V"), six months before Buchan was sworn in as viceroy. The leader of His Majesty's Loyal Opposition at the time, Mackenzie King, felt Buchan should serve as governor general as a commoner.[[113]](#cite_note-122) However, George V insisted he be represented by a peer. With the appointment of Massey as governor general in 1952, governors general ceased to be members of the peerage; successive prime ministers since that date have held to the non-binding and defeated (in 1934) principles of the 1919 [Nickle Resolution](/wiki/Canadian_titles_debate#The_Nickle_Resolution "Canadian titles debate").

Under the orders' constitutions, the governor general serves as Chancellor and Principal Companion of the [Order of Canada](/wiki/Order_of_Canada "Order of Canada"),[[114]](#cite_note-123) Chancellor of the [Order of Military Merit](/wiki/Order_of_Military_Merit_(Canada) "Order of Military Merit (Canada)"),[[115]](#cite_note-124) and Chancellor of the [Order of Merit of the Police Forces](/wiki/Order_of_Merit_of_the_Police_Forces "Order of Merit of the Police Forces").[[116]](#cite_note-125) The governor general also upon installation automatically becomes a Knight or Dame of Justice and the Prior and Chief Officer in Canada of the [Most Venerable Order of the Hospital of Saint John of Jerusalem](/wiki/Venerable_Order_of_Saint_John "Venerable Order of Saint John").[[117]](#cite_note-126) As acting commander-in-chief, the governor general is further routinely granted the [Canadian Forces' Decoration](/wiki/Canadian_Forces%27_Decoration "Canadian Forces' Decoration") by the [chief of the Defence Staff](/wiki/Chief_of_the_Defence_Staff_(Canada) "Chief of the Defence Staff (Canada)") on behalf of the monarch. All of these honours are retained following an incumbent's departure from office, with the individual remaining in the highest categories of the orders, and they may also be further distinguished with induction into other orders or the receipt of other awards.[[n 10]](#cite_note-Hons-128)

The [Viceregal Salute](/wiki/Honors_music "Honors music") — composed of the first six [bars](/wiki/Bar_(music) "Bar (music)") of the Royal Anthem ("[God Save the King](/wiki/God_Save_the_King "God Save the King")") followed by the first and last four bars of the [national anthem](/wiki/National_anthem "National anthem") ("[O Canada](/wiki/O_Canada "O Canada")") — is the [salute](/wiki/Salute "Salute") used to greet the governor general upon arrival and departure from most official events.[[119]](#cite_note-129) To mark the viceroy's presence at any building, ship, airplane, or car in Canada, the [governor general's flag](/wiki/Flag_of_the_governor_general_of_Canada "Flag of the governor general of Canada") is employed. The present form was adopted on 23 February 1981 and,[[120]](#cite_note-Flag-130) in the federal jurisdiction, takes precedence over all other flags except for the [sovereign's flag for Canada](/wiki/Royal_flags_of_Canada "Royal flags of Canada").[[121]](#cite_note-131) When the governor general undertakes a state visit, however, the [national flag](/wiki/Flag_of_Canada "Flag of Canada") is generally employed to mark governor general's presence.[[120]](#cite_note-Flag-130) This flag is also, along with all flags on Canadian Forces property, flown at half-mast upon the death of an incumbent or former governor general.[[122]](#cite_note-132)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/12/Governor_General_of_Canada_Flag_-_Drapeau_de_la_gouverneure_g%C3%A9n%C3%A9rale_du_Canada_%282138897131%29.jpg/250px-Governor_General_of_Canada_Flag_-_Drapeau_de_la_gouverneure_g%C3%A9n%C3%A9rale_du_Canada_%282138897131%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Governor_General_of_Canada_Flag_-_Drapeau_de_la_gouverneure_g%C3%A9n%C3%A9rale_du_Canada_(2138897131).jpg)

The present [flag of the governor general](/wiki/Flag_of_the_Governor_General_of_Canada "Flag of the Governor General of Canada") was adopted in 1981.

The [crest](/wiki/Crest_(heraldry) "Crest (heraldry)") of the Royal [Arms of Canada](/wiki/Arms_of_Canada "Arms of Canada") is employed as the badge of the governor general, appearing on the viceroy's flag and on other objects associated with the person or the office. This is the fourth such incarnation of the governor general's mark since confederation.[[123]](#cite_note-133)
