### French and British colonies

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Governor_General_of_Canada&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: French and British colonies")]

[French](/wiki/France "France") colonization of [North America](/wiki/North_America "North America") began in the 1580s and [Aymar de Chaste](/wiki/Aymar_Chaste "Aymar Chaste") was appointed in 1602 by King [Henry IV](/wiki/Henry_IV_of_France "Henry IV of France") as Viceroy of Canada.[[125]](#cite_note-135)[[126]](#cite_note-136) The explorer [Samuel de Champlain](/wiki/Samuel_de_Champlain "Samuel de Champlain") became the first unofficial [governor of New France](/wiki/Governor_of_New_France "Governor of New France") in the early 17th century,[[n 11]](#cite_note-GGdate-138) serving until [Charles Huault de Montmagny](/wiki/Charles_Huault_de_Montmagny "Charles Huault de Montmagny") was in 1636 formally appointed to the post by King [Louis XIII](/wiki/Louis_XIII "Louis XIII"). The French [Company of One Hundred Associates](/wiki/Company_of_One_Hundred_Associates "Company of One Hundred Associates") then administered New France until King [Louis XIV](/wiki/Louis_XIV "Louis XIV") took control of the colony and appointed [Augustin de Saffray de Mésy](/wiki/Augustin_de_Saffray_de_M%C3%A9sy "Augustin de Saffray de Mésy") as the first [governor general](/wiki/Governor_General_of_New_France "Governor General of New France") in 1663,[[128]](#cite_note-139) after whom 12 more people served in the post.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/47/Louis_de_Buade_de_Frontenac_02.jpg/250px-Louis_de_Buade_de_Frontenac_02.jpg)](/wiki/File:Louis_de_Buade_de_Frontenac_02.jpg)

Statue of [Louis de Buade de Frontenac](/wiki/Louis_de_Buade_de_Frontenac "Louis de Buade de Frontenac"), the third and sixth [Governor General of New France](/wiki/Governor_General_of_New_France "Governor General of New France"), at the [Quebec Parliament Building](/wiki/Parliament_Building_(Quebec) "Parliament Building (Quebec)").

With the signing of the [Treaty of Paris](/wiki/Treaty_of_Paris_(1763) "Treaty of Paris (1763)") in 1763, France relinquished most of its North American territories, including Canada, to [Great Britain](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Great_Britain "Kingdom of Great Britain").[[129]](#cite_note-140) King [George III](/wiki/George_III "George III") then issued in that same year [a royal proclamation](/wiki/Royal_Proclamation_of_1763 "Royal Proclamation of 1763") establishing, amongst other regulations, the Office of the Governor of Quebec to preside over the new [Province of Quebec](/wiki/Province_of_Quebec_(1763-1791) "Province of Quebec (1763-1791)").[[130]](#cite_note-141) [Nova Scotia](/wiki/Nova_Scotia "Nova Scotia") and [New Brunswick](/wiki/New_Brunswick "New Brunswick") remained completely separate colonies, each with their own governor, until the cabinet of [William Pitt](/wiki/William_Pitt_the_Younger "William Pitt the Younger") adopted in the 1780s the idea that they, along with Quebec and [Prince Edward Island](/wiki/Prince_Edward_Island "Prince Edward Island"), should have as their respective governors a single individual styled as *governor-in-chief*. The post was created in 1786, with [Guy Carleton, 1st Baron Dorchester](/wiki/Guy_Carleton,_1st_Baron_Dorchester "Guy Carleton, 1st Baron Dorchester") as its first occupant. However, the governor-in-chief directly governed only Quebec. It was not until the splitting in 1791 of the province of Quebec, to accommodate the influx of [United Empire Loyalists](/wiki/United_Empire_Loyalist "United Empire Loyalist") fleeing the [American revolutionary war](/wiki/American_Revolution "American Revolution"), that the King's representative, with a change in title to *Governor General*, directly governed [Lower Canada](/wiki/Lower_Canada "Lower Canada"), while the other three colonies were each administered by a lieutenant governor in his stead.

Following the 1783 recognition of the independence of the 13 continental colonies that became the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States") of America and the transfer of [East Florida](/wiki/East_Florida "East Florida") and [West Florida](/wiki/West_Florida "West Florida") to Spain, the remaining British colonies of North America, including [Bermuda](/wiki/Bermuda "Bermuda"), were partly integrated as [British North America](/wiki/British_North_America "British North America"). During the [War of 1812](/wiki/War_of_1812 "War of 1812"), lieutenant-general Sir [George Prevost](/wiki/George_Prevost "George Prevost") was appointed as "Captain-General and Governor-in-Chief in and Over the Provinces of Upper-Canada, Lower-Canada, Nova-Scotia, and New-Brunswick, and Their Several Dependencies, Vice-Admiral of the Same, Lieutenant-General and Commander of all His Majesty's Forces in the Said Provinces of Lower Canada and Upper-Canada, Nova-Scotia, and New-Brunswick, and Their Several Dependencies, and in the Islands of Newfoundland, Prince Edward, Cape Breton, and the Bermudas, &c. &c. &c."[[131]](#cite_note-142)

### Responsible government

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Governor_General_of_Canada&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Responsible government")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/52/United_Canada_1848.png/250px-United_Canada_1848.png)](/wiki/File:United_Canada_1848.png)

Political organization of the [Province of Canada](/wiki/Province_of_Canada "Province of Canada"), after the introduction of [responsible government](/wiki/Responsible_government "Responsible government") under the Union Act, 1848

The [Rebellions of 1837](/wiki/Rebellions_of_1837 "Rebellions of 1837") brought about great changes to the role of the governor general, prompting, as they did, the British government to grant [responsible government](/wiki/Responsible_government "Responsible government") to the Canadian provinces.[[132]](#cite_note-143)[[133]](#cite_note-144) As a result, the viceroys became largely nominal heads, while the democratically elected [legislatures](/wiki/Legislative_buildings_of_Canada "Legislative buildings of Canada") and the [premiers](/wiki/Premier_(Canada) "Premier (Canada)") they supported exercised the authority belonging to the Crown; a concept first put to the test when, in 1849, [Governor-General of the Province of Canada](/wiki/List_of_Governors_General_of_Canada#Governors_general_of_the_Province_of_Canada,_1841–1867 "List of Governors General of Canada") and Lieutenant-Governor of [Canada East](/wiki/Canada_East "Canada East") [James Bruce, 8th Earl of Elgin](/wiki/James_Bruce,_8th_Earl_of_Elgin "James Bruce, 8th Earl of Elgin") granted Royal Assent to the [Rebellion Losses Bill](/wiki/Rebellion_Losses_Bill "Rebellion Losses Bill"), despite his personal misgivings towards the legislation.[[134]](#cite_note-145)

This arrangement continued after the reunification in 1840 of Upper and Lower Canada into the [Province of Canada](/wiki/Province_of_Canada "Province of Canada"), and the establishment of the Dominion of Canada in 1867. The governor general carried out in Canada all the parliamentary and ceremonial functions of a constitutional monarch—amongst other things, granting Royal Assent, issuing Orders-in-Council, and taking advice from the [Canadian Privy Council](/wiki/Queen%27s_Privy_Council_for_Canada "Queen's Privy Council for Canada"). However, the governor still remained not a viceroy, in the true sense of the word, being still a representative of and liaison to the British government[[65]](#cite_note-Heard-72)[[135]](#cite_note-146)—the Queen in her British council of ministers—who answered to the [secretary of state for the colonies](/wiki/Secretary_of_State_for_the_Colonies "Secretary of State for the Colonies") in [London](/wiki/London "London") and who,[[136]](#cite_note-147) as a British observer of Canadian politics, held well into the [First World War](/wiki/First_World_War "First World War") a suite of offices in the [East Block](/wiki/East_Block "East Block") of [Parliament Hill](/wiki/Parliament_Hill "Parliament Hill").[[n 12]](#cite_note-EB-148) But, the new position of [Canadian high commissioner to the United Kingdom](/wiki/High_Commission_of_Canada_in_the_United_Kingdom "High Commission of Canada in the United Kingdom"), created in 1880, began to take over the governor general's role as a link between the Canadian and British governments, leaving the viceroy increasingly as a personal representative of the monarch.[[137]](#cite_note-149) As such, the governor general had to retain a sense of political neutrality; a skill that was put to the test when [John Campbell, Marquess of Lorne](/wiki/John_Campbell,_9th_Duke_of_Argyll "John Campbell, 9th Duke of Argyll"), disagreed with his Canadian prime minister, [John A. Macdonald](/wiki/John_A._Macdonald "John A. Macdonald"), over the dismissal of [Lieutenant Governor of Quebec](/wiki/Lieutenant_Governor_of_Quebec "Lieutenant Governor of Quebec") [Luc Letellier de St-Just](/wiki/Luc_Letellier_de_St-Just "Luc Letellier de St-Just"). On the advice of the colonial secretary, and to avoid conflict with the Canadian Cabinet, Campbell did eventually concede and released St-Just from duty.[[138]](#cite_note-150) The governor general was then in May 1891 called upon to resolve the dominion's first cabinet crisis, wherein Macdonald died, leaving [Lord Stanley of Preston](/wiki/Frederick_Stanley,_16th_Earl_of_Derby "Frederick Stanley, 16th Earl of Derby") to select a new prime minister.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ea/5th_Royal_Highlanders-_inspection_by_Governor_General_at_Champ_de_Mars%2C_Montreal%2C_August_1914_%2819533676175%29.jpg/250px-5th_Royal_Highlanders-_inspection_by_Governor_General_at_Champ_de_Mars%2C_Montreal%2C_August_1914_%2819533676175%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:5th_Royal_Highlanders-_inspection_by_Governor_General_at_Champ_de_Mars,_Montreal,_August_1914_(19533676175).jpg)

Governor General [Prince Arthur](/wiki/Prince_Arthur,_Duke_of_Connaught_and_Strathearn "Prince Arthur, Duke of Connaught and Strathearn") inspects members of the [Canadian Expeditionary Force](/wiki/Canadian_Expeditionary_Force "Canadian Expeditionary Force") at [Valcartier Camp](/wiki/Valcartier_Camp "Valcartier Camp"), 1914

As early as 1880, the viceregal family and court attracted minor ridicule from the Queen's subjects: in July of that year, someone under the [pseudonym](/wiki/Pseudonym "Pseudonym") *Captain Mac* included in a pamphlet called *Canada: from the Lakes to the Gulf*, a coarse satire of an investiture ceremony at Rideau Hall, in which a retired inn-keeper and his wife undergo the rigorous protocol of the royal household and sprawl on the floor before the [Duke of Argyll](/wiki/John_Campbell,_9th_Duke_of_Argyll "John Campbell, 9th Duke of Argyll") so as to be granted the knighthood for which they had "paid in cold, hard cash".[[139]](#cite_note-151) Later, prior to the arrival of [Prince Arthur, Duke of Connaught and Strathearn](/wiki/Prince_Arthur,_Duke_of_Connaught_and_Strathearn "Prince Arthur, Duke of Connaught and Strathearn") (the uncle of King George V), to take up the post of governor general, there was a "feeble undercurrent of criticism" centring on worries about a rigid court at Rideau Hall; worries that turned out to be unfounded as the royal couple was actually more relaxed than their predecessors.[[140]](#cite_note-152)

### Emerging nationality to an independent kingdom

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Governor_General_of_Canada&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Emerging nationality to an independent kingdom")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/dc/Governor_General_of_Canada_is_welcomed_to_Washington_by_Secretary_of_State_Kellogg._Attended_by_all_the_pomp_and_ceremony_a_republic_can_bestow%2C_Governor_General_Willingdon_of_Canada_was_LCCN2016888558.tif/lossy-page1-250px-thumbnail.tif.jpg)](/wiki/File:Governor_General_of_Canada_is_welcomed_to_Washington_by_Secretary_of_State_Kellogg._Attended_by_all_the_pomp_and_ceremony_a_republic_can_bestow,_Governor_General_Willingdon_of_Canada_was_LCCN2016888558.tif)

[Marquess of Willingdon](/wiki/Freeman_Freeman-Thomas,_1st_Marquess_of_Willingdon "Freeman Freeman-Thomas, 1st Marquess of Willingdon") (left) during an [official visit](/wiki/Official_visit "Official visit") to [Washington, D.C.](/wiki/Washington,_D.C. "Washington, D.C.") as the governor general of Canada.

During the [First World War](/wiki/First_World_War "First World War"), into which Canada was drawn due to its association with the United Kingdom, the governor general's role turned from one of cultural patron and state ceremony to one of military inspector and morale booster. Starting in 1914, governor general Prince Arthur donned his [field marshal](/wiki/Field_Marshal "Field Marshal")'s uniform and put his efforts into raising contingents, inspecting army camps, and seeing troops off before their voyage to Europe. These actions, however, led to conflict with the prime minister at the time, [Robert Borden](/wiki/Robert_Borden "Robert Borden"); though the latter placed blame on military secretary Edward Stanton, he also opined that the Duke "laboured under the handicap of his position as a member of the Royal Family and never realized his limitations as Governor General".[[141]](#cite_note-153) Prince Arthur's successor, [Victor Cavendish, 9th Duke of Devonshire](/wiki/Victor_Cavendish,_9th_Duke_of_Devonshire "Victor Cavendish, 9th Duke of Devonshire"), faced the [Conscription Crisis of 1917](/wiki/Conscription_Crisis_of_1917 "Conscription Crisis of 1917") and held discussions with his Canadian prime minister, as well as members of the official opposition, on the matter. Once the government implemented conscription, Devonshire, after consulting on the pulse of the nation with Sir [Wilfrid Laurier](/wiki/Wilfrid_Laurier "Wilfrid Laurier"), Vincent Massey, [Henri Bourassa](/wiki/Henri_Bourassa "Henri Bourassa"), [Archbishop of Montreal](/wiki/Archbishop_of_Montreal "Archbishop of Montreal") [Paul Bruchési](/wiki/Paul_Bruch%C3%A9si "Paul Bruchési"), [Duncan Campbell Scott](/wiki/Duncan_Campbell_Scott "Duncan Campbell Scott"), [Vilhjalmur Stefansson](/wiki/Vilhjalmur_Stefansson "Vilhjalmur Stefansson"), and [Stephen Leacock](/wiki/Stephen_Leacock "Stephen Leacock"), made efforts to conciliate [Quebec](/wiki/Quebec "Quebec"), though he had little real success.[[142]](#cite_note-154)

Canada's national sentiment had gained fortitude through the country's sacrifices on the battlefields of the First World War and, by war's end, the interference of the British government in Canadian affairs was causing ever-increasing discontent amongst Canadian officials;[[n 13]](#cite_note-Adv-156) In 1918, *[The Toronto Star](/wiki/The_Toronto_Star "The Toronto Star")* was even advocating the end of the office.[[144]](#cite_note-157) The governor general's role was also changing to focus less on the larger Empire and more on uniquely Canadian affairs,[[n 14]](#cite_note-Pons-159) including the undertaking of official international visits on behalf of Canada, the first being that of the [Marquess of Willingdon](/wiki/Freeman_Freeman-Thomas,_1st_Marquess_of_Willingdon "Freeman Freeman-Thomas, 1st Marquess of Willingdon") to the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States"), where he was accorded by [President](/wiki/President_of_the_United_States "President of the United States") [Calvin Coolidge](/wiki/Calvin_Coolidge "Calvin Coolidge") the full honours of representative of a head of state.[[n 15]](#cite_note-US-160)[[11]](#cite_note-Hubbard166-12) It would be another decade, however, before the King-Byng Affair: another catalyst for change in the relationship between Canada—indeed, all the dominions—and the United Kingdom, and thus the purpose of the governor general.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1b/Lord-byng.jpg/250px-Lord-byng.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lord-byng.jpg)

[Viscount Byng of Vimy](/wiki/Julian_Byng,_1st_Viscount_Byng_of_Vimy "Julian Byng, 1st Viscount Byng of Vimy"), who was involved in the [King–Byng affair](/wiki/King%E2%80%93Byng_affair "King–Byng affair"), a catalyst for change over the role of the governor general in the British Empire

In 1926, prime minister Mackenzie King, facing a [no-confidence vote](/wiki/Motion_of_no_confidence "Motion of no confidence") in the House of Commons over a scandal in his party, requested that governor general Byng dissolve parliament and call an election. Byng, however, refused his Canadian prime minister's advice, citing both the facts that King held the [minority of seats](/wiki/Minority_government "Minority government") in the house and that a general election had been held only months earlier; he thus called on [Arthur Meighen](/wiki/Arthur_Meighen "Arthur Meighen") to [form a government](/wiki/Kissing_Hands "Kissing Hands"). Within a week however, Meighen's [Conservative](/wiki/Conservative_Party_of_Canada_(historical) "Conservative Party of Canada (historical)") government lost its own non-confidence vote, forcing the Governor General to dissolve parliament and call elections that saw Mackenzie King returned to power.[[146]](#cite_note-161) King then went on to the [Imperial Conference](/wiki/1926_Imperial_Conference "1926 Imperial Conference") that same year and there pushed for reorganizations that resulted in the [Balfour Declaration](/wiki/Balfour_Declaration_of_1926 "Balfour Declaration of 1926"), which declared formally the practical reality that had existed for some years: namely, that the dominions were fully autonomous and equal in status to the United Kingdom.[[147]](#cite_note-162) These new developments were codified in the [Statute of Westminster](/wiki/Statute_of_Westminster_1931 "Statute of Westminster 1931"), through the enactment of which on 11 December 1931, Canada, along with the [Union of South Africa](/wiki/Union_of_South_Africa "Union of South Africa") and the [Irish Free State](/wiki/Irish_Free_State "Irish Free State"), immediately obtained formal legislative independence from the UK.[[148]](#cite_note-163) In addition, the Balfour Declaration also held that the governor general would cease to act as the representative of the British government. Accordingly, in 1928, the United Kingdom appointed its first [High Commissioner to Canada](/wiki/List_of_High_Commissioners_of_the_United_Kingdom_to_Canada "List of High Commissioners of the United Kingdom to Canada") thus effectively ending the governor general's diplomatic role as the British government's envoy.[[149]](#cite_note-164)

The governor general thus became solely the representative of the monarch within Canadian jurisdiction, ceasing completely to be an agent of the British Cabinet,[[n 16]](#cite_note-Balf-166)[[8]](#cite_note-DCH3-9)[[151]](#cite_note-167) and as such would be appointed only on the advice of his Canadian prime minister.[[152]](#cite_note-168) The Canadian Cabinet's first recommendation under this new system was still, however, a [British subject](/wiki/British_subject "British subject") born outside of Canada, John Buchan (later Lord Tweedsmuir).

Tweedsmuir's birthplace aside, though, the professional author took further than any of his predecessors the idea of a distinct Canadian identity,[[153]](#cite_note-169) travelling the length and breadth of the country, including, for the first time for a governor general, the [Arctic](/wiki/Canadian_Arctic "Canadian Arctic") regions.[[154]](#cite_note-170) Not all Canadians, however, shared Tweedsmuir's views; the baron raised the ire of [imperialists](/wiki/Imperialism "Imperialism") when he said in Montreal in 1937: "a Canadian's first loyalty is not to the British [Commonwealth of Nations](/wiki/Commonwealth_of_Nations "Commonwealth of Nations"), but to Canada and Canada's King",[[155]](#cite_note-171) a statement the *[Montreal Gazette](/wiki/Montreal_Gazette "Montreal Gazette")* dubbed as "disloyal".[[156]](#cite_note-172) During Tweedsmuir's time as viceroy, which started in 1935, calls began to emerge for a Canadian-born individual to be appointed as governor general; but Tweedsmuir died suddenly in office in 1940, while Canada was in the midst of the [Second World War](/wiki/Second_World_War "Second World War"), and Mackenzie King did not feel it was the right time to search for a suitable Canadian.[[157]](#cite_note-173) The [Earl of Athlone](/wiki/Alexander_Cambridge,_1st_Earl_of_Athlone "Alexander Cambridge, 1st Earl of Athlone") was instead appointed by King George VI, Athlone's nephew, to be his viceroy for the duration of the war.

### Quebec nationalism and constitutional patriation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Governor_General_of_Canada&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Quebec nationalism and constitutional patriation")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6e/Portrait_of_Governor_General_Georges_Vanier_%281965_UPI_press_photo%29.jpg/250px-Portrait_of_Governor_General_Georges_Vanier_%281965_UPI_press_photo%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Portrait_of_Governor_General_Georges_Vanier_(1965_UPI_press_photo).jpg)

[Georges Vanier](/wiki/Georges_Vanier "Georges Vanier"), the 19th governor general of Canada. The convention of alternating between francophones and anglophones began with Vanier's appointment.

It was in 1952, a mere five days before King George VI's death, that [Vincent Massey](/wiki/Vincent_Massey "Vincent Massey") became the first Canadian-born person to be appointed as a governor general in Canada since the [Marquis de Vaudreuil-Cavagnal](/wiki/Pierre_Fran%C3%A7ois_de_Rigaud,_Marquis_de_Vaudreuil-Cavagnal "Pierre François de Rigaud, Marquis de Vaudreuil-Cavagnal") was made governor general of New France on 1 January 1755, as well as the first not to be elevated to the [peerage](/wiki/Peerage "Peerage") since Sir [Edmund Walker Head](/wiki/Edmund_Walker_Head "Edmund Walker Head") in 1854. There was some trepidation about this departure from tradition and Massey was intended to be a compromise: he was known to embody loyalty, dignity, and formality, as expected from a viceroy.[[158]](#cite_note-174)

As his viceregal tenure neared an end, it was thought that Massey, an [anglophone](/wiki/English_language "English language"), should be followed by a [francophone](/wiki/Francophone "Francophone") Canadian; and so, in spite of his [Liberal Party](/wiki/Liberal_Party_of_Canada "Liberal Party of Canada") attachments, [Georges Vanier](/wiki/Georges_Vanier "Georges Vanier") was chosen by [Conservative](/wiki/Conservative_Party_of_Canada "Conservative Party of Canada") prime minister [John Diefenbaker](/wiki/John_Diefenbaker "John Diefenbaker") as the next governor general. Vanier was subsequently appointed by Queen [Elizabeth II](/wiki/Elizabeth_II "Elizabeth II"), in person, at a meeting of her Canadian Cabinet,[[159]](#cite_note-175) thus initiating the convention of alternating between individuals from [Canada's two main linguistic groups](/wiki/Languages_of_Canada "Languages of Canada"). This move did not, however, placate those who were fostering the new [Quebec nationalist movement](/wiki/Quebec_nationalism "Quebec nationalism"), for whom the monarchy and other federal institutions were a target for attack. Though Vanier was a native of Quebec and fostered biculturalism, he was not immune to the barbs of [the province's sovereigntists](/wiki/Quebec_sovereignty_movement "Quebec sovereignty movement") and, when he attended *[la Fête St-Jean-Baptiste](/wiki/National_Holiday_(Quebec) "National Holiday (Quebec)")* in [Montreal](/wiki/Montreal "Montreal") in 1964, a group of separatists held placards reading "**Vanier vendu**" ("Vanier sold out") and "**Vanier fou de la Reine**" ("Vanier Queen's jester").[[160]](#cite_note-176)

In light of this regional nationalism and a resultant change in attitudes towards Canadian identity, images and the role of the monarchy were cautiously downplayed, and Vanier's successor, [Roland Michener](/wiki/Roland_Michener "Roland Michener"), was the last viceroy to practice many of the office's ancient traditions, such as the wearing of [court uniform](/wiki/Court_uniform_and_dress_in_the_United_Kingdom "Court uniform and dress in the United Kingdom") by the governor general, the requirement of [court dress](/wiki/Court_uniform_and_dress_in_the_United_Kingdom "Court uniform and dress in the United Kingdom") for state occasions, and expecting women to [curtsey](/wiki/Curtsey "Curtsey") before the governor general.[[161]](#cite_note-GGMich-177) At the same time, he initiated new practices for the viceroy, including regular conferences with the lieutenant governors and the undertaking of [state visits](/wiki/State_visits "State visits").[[161]](#cite_note-GGMich-177) He presided over [Canada's centennial celebrations](/wiki/Canadian_Centennial "Canadian Centennial") and the coincidental [Expo 67](/wiki/Expo_67 "Expo 67"), to which [French president](/wiki/President_of_France "President of France") [Charles de Gaulle](/wiki/Charles_de_Gaulle "Charles de Gaulle") was invited. Michener was with de Gaulle when he made his infamous "**[Vive le Québec libre](/wiki/Vive_le_Qu%C3%A9bec_libre "Vive le Québec libre")**" speech in [Montreal](/wiki/Montreal "Montreal") and was cheered wildly by the gathered crowd while they booed and jeered Michener.[[162]](#cite_note-178) With the additional recognition of the monarchy as a Canadian institution,[[163]](#cite_note-179)[[164]](#cite_note-180) the establishment of a distinct [Canadian honours system](/wiki/Orders,_decorations,_and_medals_of_Canada "Orders, decorations, and medals of Canada"), an increase of state visits coming with Canada's growing role on the world stage, and the more prevalent use of [television](/wiki/Television "Television") to visually broadcast ceremonial state affairs, the governor general became more publicly active in national life.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/12/Jeanne_Sauv%C3%A9_1985.jpg/250px-Jeanne_Sauv%C3%A9_1985.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jeanne_Sauv%C3%A9_1985.jpg)

[Jeanne Sauvé](/wiki/Jeanne_Sauv%C3%A9 "Jeanne Sauvé") (left), Canada's first female governor general, with a guardsman (private) of the Canadian Grenadier Guards of Montreal

The Cabinet in June 1978 proposed the constitutional amendment Bill C-60, that, amongst other changes, vested executive authority directly in the governor general and renamed the position as *First Canadian*,[[165]](#cite_note-181)[[166]](#cite_note-182)[[167]](#cite_note-Pepall-183) but the proposal was thwarted by the provincial premiers.[[168]](#cite_note-184)[[169]](#cite_note-185)[[170]](#cite_note-186) When the [constitution was patriated](/wiki/Patriation "Patriation") four years later, the new amending formula for the documents outlined that any changes to the Crown, including the Office of the Governor General, would require the consent of all the provincial legislatures plus the federal parliament.[[171]](#cite_note-187) By 1984, Canada's first female governor general, [Jeanne Sauvé](/wiki/Jeanne_Sauv%C3%A9 "Jeanne Sauvé"), was appointed. While it was she who created the [Canadian Heraldic Authority](/wiki/Canadian_Heraldic_Authority "Canadian Heraldic Authority"), as permitted by letters patent from Queen Elizabeth II, and who championed youth and world peace, Sauvé proved to be a controversial vicereine, closing to the public the grounds of the Queen's residence and self-aggrandizingly breaching protocol on a number of occasions.[[167]](#cite_note-Pepall-183)[[172]](#cite_note-188)[[173]](#cite_note-189)

### Withering and renaissance

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Governor_General_of_Canada&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Withering and renaissance")]

[Sarah Ferguson](/wiki/Sarah_Ferguson "Sarah Ferguson"), said in 2009 that sometime during her marriage to the then Prince Andrew, Duke of York (later [Andrew Mountbatten-Windsor](/wiki/Andrew_Mountbatten-Windsor "Andrew Mountbatten-Windsor")), her husband was offered the position of governor general of Canada, and she speculated in hindsight that their agreement to refuse the commission may have been a contributing factor in their eventual break-up.[[174]](#cite_note-190) Instead, Sauvé's tenure as governor general was book-ended by a series of appointments—[Edward Schreyer](/wiki/Edward_Schreyer "Edward Schreyer"), [Ray Hnatyshyn](/wiki/Ray_Hnatyshyn "Ray Hnatyshyn"), and [Roméo LeBlanc](/wiki/Rom%C3%A9o_LeBlanc "Roméo LeBlanc")—that have been generally regarded as mere [patronage](/wiki/Patronage "Patronage") postings for former politicians and friends of the incumbent prime minister at the time,[[n 17]](#cite_note-LeBlanc-192)[[94]](#cite_note-Boyce-103)[[167]](#cite_note-Pepall-183) and despite the duties they carried out, their combined time in the viceregal office is generally viewed as unremarkable at best, and damaging to the office at worst.[[94]](#cite_note-Boyce-103)[[167]](#cite_note-Pepall-183)[[99]](#cite_note-Toffoli-108)[[175]](#cite_note-CMNLeB-191)[[176]](#cite_note-Martin-193) As David Smith described it: "Notwithstanding the personal qualities of the appointees, which have often been extraordinary, the Canadian governor general has become a hermetic head of state—ignored by press, politicians and public."[[177]](#cite_note-194) It was theorized by Peter Boyce that this was due, in part, to widespread misunderstanding about the governor general's role coupled with a lack of public presence compared to the media coverage dedicated to the increasingly presidentialized prime minister.[[94]](#cite_note-Boyce-103)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d4/Vladimir_Putin_in_Canada_18-19_December_2000-7.jpg/250px-Vladimir_Putin_in_Canada_18-19_December_2000-7.jpg)](/wiki/File:Vladimir_Putin_in_Canada_18-19_December_2000-7.jpg)

Governor General [Adrienne Clarkson](/wiki/Adrienne_Clarkson "Adrienne Clarkson") (right) toasts [Russian president](/wiki/President_of_Russia "President of Russia") [Vladimir Putin](/wiki/Vladimir_Putin "Vladimir Putin") in the ballroom of Rideau Hall, 18 December 2000

It was with the Queen's appointment of [Adrienne Clarkson](/wiki/Adrienne_Clarkson "Adrienne Clarkson"), on the advice of prime minister [Jean Chrétien](/wiki/Jean_Chr%C3%A9tien "Jean Chrétien"), that a shift in the office took place. Clarkson was the first Canadian viceroy to have not previously held any political or military position—coming as she did from a background of television journalism with the [Canadian Broadcasting Corporation](/wiki/Canadian_Broadcasting_Corporation "Canadian Broadcasting Corporation")—was the first since 1952 to have been born outside of Canada, the first from a [visible minority](/wiki/Visible_minority "Visible minority") (she is of Chinese ancestry), and, by her being accompanied to Rideau Hall by her husband, author and philosopher [John Ralston Saul](/wiki/John_Ralston_Saul "John Ralston Saul"), the official appointment brought an unofficial pair to the viceregal placement,[[178]](#cite_note-195)[[179]](#cite_note-196) in that the governor general would not be the only person actively exploring Canadian theory and culture. Clarkson managed to bring the viceregal office back into the collective consciousness of Canadians, winning praise for touring the country more than any of her predecessors, her inspiring speeches, and her dedication to the military in her role as the commander-in-chief's representative.[[180]](#cite_note-197)[[181]](#cite_note-CMNSP05-198)[[182]](#cite_note-199)[[183]](#cite_note-200)[[184]](#cite_note-201)[[185]](#cite_note-202) This did not come without a cost, however, as the attention also drew widespread criticism of the governor general's increased spending on state affairs, for which the office was symbolically rebuked by parliament when it voted in favour of cutting by 10% the viceregal budget it had earlier supported,[[186]](#cite_note-203)[[187]](#cite_note-204) as well as for fostering the notion, through various demonstrations, that the governor general was ultimately the Canadian head of state above the Queen herself,[[188]](#cite_note-205)[[189]](#cite_note-206)[[190]](#cite_note-FraserNP-207) an approach that was said by [Jack Granatstein](/wiki/Jack_Granatstein "Jack Granatstein") to have caused "a fury" with the Queen on one occasion in 2004.[[191]](#cite_note-208) This attitude was not unique to Clarkson, though; it had been observed that, for some decades, staff at Rideau Hall and various government departments in Ottawa had been pushing to present the governor general as head of state,[[192]](#cite_note-209) part of a wider Liberal policy on the monarchy that had been in effect at least since the proposed constitutional changes in the 1970s,[[167]](#cite_note-Pepall-183) if not the [1964 Truncheon Saturday riot](/wiki/History_of_monarchy_in_Canada#Turbulent_decades "History of monarchy in Canada") in Quebec City.[[190]](#cite_note-FraserNP-207) Indeed, international observers opined that the viceroys had been, over the years, making deliberate attempts to distance themselves from the sovereign, for fear of being too closely associated with any "Britishness" the monarch embodied.[[94]](#cite_note-Boyce-103)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f0/Barack_Obama_%26_Micha%C3%ABlle_Jean_2-19-09.jpg/250px-Barack_Obama_%26_Micha%C3%ABlle_Jean_2-19-09.jpg)](/wiki/File:Barack_Obama_%26_Micha%C3%ABlle_Jean_2-19-09.jpg)

As the representative of Canada's head of state, the governor general, [Michaëlle Jean](/wiki/Micha%C3%ABlle_Jean "Michaëlle Jean"), welcomes [US President](/wiki/President_of_the_United_States "President of the United States") [Barack Obama](/wiki/Barack_Obama "Barack Obama") to Canada, 19 February 2009

Prime minister [Paul Martin](/wiki/Paul_Martin "Paul Martin") followed Chrétien's example and, for Clarkson's successor, put forward to the Queen the name of [Michaëlle Jean](/wiki/Micha%C3%ABlle_Jean "Michaëlle Jean"), who was, like Clarkson, a woman, a refugee, a member of a visible minority, a CBC career journalist, and married to an intellectual husband who worked in the arts.[[193]](#cite_note-210) Her appointment initially sparked accusations that she was a supporter of Quebec sovereignty, and it was observed that she had on a few occasions trodden into political matters,[[194]](#cite_note-211)[[195]](#cite_note-212)[[196]](#cite_note-213) as well as continuing to foster the notion that the governor general had replaced the Queen as head of state, thereby "unbalancing ... the federalist symmetry".[[197]](#cite_note-214) But Jean ultimately won plaudits,[[176]](#cite_note-Martin-193) particularly for her solidarity with the Canadian Forces and the [indigenous peoples in Canada](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples_in_Canada "Indigenous peoples in Canada"), as well as her role in the [parliamentary dispute that took place between December 2008 and January 2009](/wiki/2008%E2%80%932009_Canadian_parliamentary_dispute "2008–2009 Canadian parliamentary dispute").[[198]](#cite_note-215)[[199]](#cite_note-216)[[200]](#cite_note-217)

With the appointment of academic [David Johnston](/wiki/David_Johnston_(governor_general) "David Johnston (governor general)"), former principal of [McGill University](/wiki/McGill_University "McGill University") and subsequently president of the [University of Waterloo](/wiki/University_of_Waterloo "University of Waterloo"), there was a signalled emphasis for the governor general to vigorously promote learning and innovation. Johnston stated in his inaugural address: "[We want to be] a society that innovates, embraces its talent and uses the knowledge of each of its citizens to improve the human condition for all."[[201]](#cite_note-218) There was also a recognition of Johnston's expertise in [constitutional law](/wiki/Constitutional_law "Constitutional law"), following the controversial [prorogations of Parliament](/wiki/Prorogation_in_Canada "Prorogation in Canada") in 2008 and 2009, which initiated some debate about the governor general's role as the representative of Canada's head of state.[[202]](#cite_note-219)

In late 2021, the Office of the Secretary to the Governor General confirmed its internal network was breached in a "sophisticated cyber incident".[[203]](#cite_note-220)[[204]](#cite_note-CityNewsSecBreach-221)[[205]](#cite_note-TorStarSecBreach-222)[[206]](#cite_note-CBCSecBreach-223) Officials were unable to determine the extent of the information that was accessed.[[206]](#cite_note-CBCSecBreach-223) Former security officials speculated that another country may be responsible.[[204]](#cite_note-CityNewsSecBreach-221)[[205]](#cite_note-TorStarSecBreach-222) The Canadian Centre for Cyber Security, a branch of the [Communications Security Establishment](/wiki/Communications_Security_Establishment "Communications Security Establishment"), is investigating the incident.[[206]](#cite_note-CBCSecBreach-223)

## Activities post-retirement

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Governor_General_of_Canada&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Activities post-retirement")]

Retired governors general usually either withdraw from public life or go on to hold other public offices. [Edward Schreyer](/wiki/Edward_Schreyer "Edward Schreyer"), for instance, was appointed [Canadian High Commissioner to Australia](/wiki/High_Commission_of_Canada,_Canberra "High Commission of Canada, Canberra") upon his departure from the viceregal role in 1984, and Michaëlle Jean became the [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO") [special envoy](/wiki/Special_envoy "Special envoy") to Haiti and, later, the secretary-general of [La Francophonie](/wiki/Organisation_internationale_de_la_Francophonie "Organisation internationale de la Francophonie").[[207]](#cite_note-224) Schreyer also become the first former governor general to run for elected office in Canada when he unsuccessfully vied for a seat in the House of Commons as a [New Democratic Party](/wiki/New_Democratic_Party_(Canada) "New Democratic Party (Canada)") candidate. Prior to 1952, several former viceroys returned to political careers in the United Kingdom, sitting with party affiliations in the House of Lords and, in some cases, taking a position in the [British Cabinet](/wiki/Cabinet_of_the_United_Kingdom "Cabinet of the United Kingdom").[[n 18]](#cite_note-Post-225) The [Marquess of Lorne](/wiki/John_Campbell,_9th_Duke_of_Argyll "John Campbell, 9th Duke of Argyll") was elected a [Member of Parliament](/wiki/House_of_Commons_of_the_United_Kingdom "House of Commons of the United Kingdom") in the United Kingdom in 1895, and remained so until he became the Duke of Argyll and took his seat in the House of Lords. Others were made governors in other countries or territories: [Charles Stanley Monck, 4th Viscount Monck](/wiki/Charles_Stanley_Monck,_4th_Viscount_Monck "Charles Stanley Monck, 4th Viscount Monck") was appointed [Lord Lieutenant of Dublin](/wiki/Lord_Lieutenant_of_Dublin "Lord Lieutenant of Dublin"), the [Earl of Aberdeen](/wiki/John_Hamilton-Gordon,_1st_Marquess_of_Aberdeen_and_Temair "John Hamilton-Gordon, 1st Marquess of Aberdeen and Temair") was appointed [Lord Lieutenant of Ireland](/wiki/Lord_Lieutenant_of_Ireland "Lord Lieutenant of Ireland"), and the [Earl of Dufferin](/wiki/Frederick_Hamilton-Temple-Blackwood,_1st_Marquess_of_Dufferin_and_Ava "Frederick Hamilton-Temple-Blackwood, 1st Marquess of Dufferin and Ava"), [Henry Petty-Fitzmaurice, 5th Marquess of Lansdowne](/wiki/Henry_Petty-Fitzmaurice,_5th_Marquess_of_Lansdowne "Henry Petty-Fitzmaurice, 5th Marquess of Lansdowne"), [Gilbert Elliot-Murray-Kynynmound, 4th Earl of Minto](/wiki/Gilbert_Elliot-Murray-Kynynmound,_4th_Earl_of_Minto "Gilbert Elliot-Murray-Kynynmound, 4th Earl of Minto"), and the [Earl of Willingdon](/wiki/Freeman_Freeman-Thomas,_1st_Marquess_of_Willingdon "Freeman Freeman-Thomas, 1st Marquess of Willingdon") all subsequently served as [Viceroy of India](/wiki/Viceroy_of_India "Viceroy of India").

An outgoing governor general may leave [an eponymous award](/wiki/List_of_awards_named_after_Governors_General_of_Canada "List of awards named after Governors General of Canada") as a legacy, such as the [Stanley Cup](/wiki/Stanley_Cup "Stanley Cup"), the [Clarkson Cup](/wiki/Clarkson_Cup "Clarkson Cup"), the [Vanier Cup](/wiki/Vanier_Cup "Vanier Cup"), or the [Grey Cup](/wiki/Grey_Cup "Grey Cup"). They may found an institution, as Vanier did with the [Vanier Institute of the Family](/wiki/Vanier_Institute_of_the_Family "Vanier Institute of the Family") and Clarkson with the Institute for Canadian Citizenship. Three former governors general have released memoirs: Lord Tweedsmuir (*Memory Hold-the-Door*), Massey (*What's Past is Prologue*), and Clarkson (*Heart Matters*).

As of 2021, former governors general are entitled to a lifetime pension of nearly $150,000 and also to claim an additional $206,000 in expenses each year.[[208]](#cite_note-226)

## Spelling of the title

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Governor_General_of_Canada&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Spelling of the title")]

The letters patent constituting the office and official publications of the [government of Canada](/wiki/Government_of_Canada "Government of Canada") spell the title *governor general*, without a [hyphen](/wiki/Hyphen "Hyphen"), unlike in the other [Commonwealth realms](/wiki/Commonwealth_realms "Commonwealth realms"), which do include a hyphen.[[14]](#cite_note-GVII-15)[[214]](#cite_note-232) As *[governor](/wiki/Governor "Governor")* is the [noun](/wiki/Noun "Noun"), the title is [pluralized](/wiki/Plural "Plural") as *governors general*, rather than *governor generals*.

## Notes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Governor_General_of_Canada&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Notes")]

1. **[^](#cite_ref-1)** When the position is held by a man, the French title is *Gouverneur général du Canada*.
2. **[^](#cite_ref-Term1_25-0)** [Georges Vanier](/wiki/Georges_Vanier "Georges Vanier") served as governor general between 15 September 1959 and 5 March 1967 and [Roland Michener](/wiki/Roland_Michener "Roland Michener") served for just under seven years, from 17 April 1967 to 14 January 1974.
3. **[^](#cite_ref-Term3_26-0)** Lord Tweedsmuir died at the [Montreal Neurological Institute and Hospital](/wiki/Montreal_Neurological_Institute "Montreal Neurological Institute") on 11 February 1940 and [Georges Vanier](/wiki/Georges_Vanier "Georges Vanier") died at [Rideau Hall](/wiki/Rideau_Hall "Rideau Hall") on 5 March 1967.
4. **[^](#cite_ref-Term2_27-0)** [Roméo LeBlanc](/wiki/Rom%C3%A9o_LeBlanc "Roméo LeBlanc") resigned the viceregal post in 1999 and [Julie Payette](/wiki/Julie_Payette "Julie Payette") resigned in 2021.
5. **[^](#cite_ref-Alex_29-0)** As Alexander was to be appointed to the [British Cabinet](/wiki/Cabinet_of_the_United_Kingdom "Cabinet of the United Kingdom") and the announcement made while he was still in Canada, the Canadian Cabinet felt it necessary to end Alexander's service as viceroy immediately, while sparing him the indignity of resignation.[[24]](#cite_note-GoC106-28)
6. **[^](#cite_ref-Admin_30-0)** The only individuals to serve as administrators of the government of Canada due to the deaths in office of governors general were Chief Justice Sir [Lyman Poore Duff](/wiki/Lyman_Poore_Duff "Lyman Poore Duff") in 1940 and Chief Justice [Robert Taschereau](/wiki/Robert_Taschereau "Robert Taschereau") in 1967.
7. **[^](#cite_ref-GGMon_70-0)** Governor General the Lord Tweedsmuir said of King George VI being in the Senate in 1939 to grant Royal Assent to bills: "[When the King of Canada is present] I cease to exist as Viceroy, and retain only a shadowy legal existence as Governor General in Council."[[63]](#cite_note-69)
8. **[^](#cite_ref-RP_91-0)** See [Note 2](/wiki/King%27s_Privy_Council_for_Canada#cite_note-RP-13 "King's Privy Council for Canada") at [King's Privy Council for Canada](/wiki/King%27s_Privy_Council_for_Canada "King's Privy Council for Canada").
9. **[^](#cite_ref-95)** Robert Borden said, "it would be an absolute mistake to regard the governor general as a mere figurehead, a mere rubber stamp. During nine years of premiership, I had the opportunity of realizing how helpful may be the advice and counsel of a governor general in matters of delicacy and difficulty".[[86]](#cite_note-94)
10. **[^](#cite_ref-Hons_128-0)** Some seven years after he left office, the Earl Alexander of Tunis was appointed as a Member of the [Order of Merit](/wiki/Order_of_Merit "Order of Merit"). Similarly, Massey was awarded the [Royal Victorian Chain](/wiki/Royal_Victorian_Chain "Royal Victorian Chain") by Queen Elizabeth II approximately six months after leaving the viceregal post and was in 1967 invested into the Order of Canada.[[118]](#cite_note-127) ichener was presented with the Royal Victorian Chain a few months before he retired as governor general.
11. **[^](#cite_ref-GGdate_138-0)** [Kevin MacLeod](/wiki/Kevin_S._MacLeod "Kevin S. MacLeod"), in his book *A Crown of Maples*, pegs the start date of Champlain's governorship at 1627,[[3]](#cite_note-MacLeod34-4) whereas the official website of the Governor General of Canada puts it at 1608.[[127]](#cite_note-137)
12. **[^](#cite_ref-EB_148-0)** The offices were subsequently incorporated into the [Prime Minister's Office](/wiki/Office_of_the_Prime_Minister_(Canada) "Office of the Prime Minister (Canada)") (PMO), but have been restored to their 19th century appearance after the PMO moved to the [Langevin Block](/wiki/Langevin_Block "Langevin Block") in the 1970s, and are now preserved as a tourist attraction along with other historic offices in the East Block.[[6]](#cite_note-GGParl-7)
13. **[^](#cite_ref-Adv_156-0)** The appointment in 1916 of the Duke of Devonshire as governor general caused political problems, as Canadian prime minister Robert Borden had, counter to established common practice, not been consulted on the matter by his British counterpart, [H. H. Asquith](/wiki/H._H._Asquith "H. H. Asquith").[[143]](#cite_note-155)
14. **[^](#cite_ref-Pons_159-0)** During the [Great Depression](/wiki/Great_Depression "Great Depression"), the [Earl of Bessborough](/wiki/Vere_Ponsonby,_9th_Earl_of_Bessborough "Vere Ponsonby, 9th Earl of Bessborough") voluntarily cut his salary by ten percent as a sign of his solidarity with the Canadian people.[[145]](#cite_note-158)
15. **[^](#cite_ref-US_160-0)** Governors general had been venturing to [Washington](/wiki/Washington,_D.C. "Washington, D.C.") to meet informally with the [President of the United States](/wiki/President_of_the_United_States "President of the United States") since the time of [Viscount Monck](/wiki/Charles_Monck,_4th_Viscount_Monck "Charles Monck, 4th Viscount Monck").
16. **[^](#cite_ref-Balf_166-0)** The ministers in attendance at the Imperial Conference agreed that: "In our opinion it is an essential consequence of the equality of status existing among the members of the British Commonwealth of Nations that the Governor General of a Dominion is the representative of the Crown, holding in all essential respects the same position in relation to the administration of public affairs in the Dominion as is held by His Majesty the King in Great Britain, and that he is not the representative or agent of His Majesty's Government in Great Britain or of any Department of that Government."[[150]](#cite_note-165)
17. **[^](#cite_ref-LeBlanc_192-0)** LeBlanc's strong ties to the Liberal Party led other party leaders to protest his appointment by boycotting his installation ceremony.[[175]](#cite_note-CMNLeB-191)
18. **[^](#cite_ref-Post_225-0)** In 1952, Earl Alexander of Tunis resigned as governor general of Canada to accept an appointment as [Minister of Defence](/wiki/Minister_of_Defence_(UK) "Minister of Defence (UK)") in the Cabinet of [Winston Churchill](/wiki/Winston_Churchill "Winston Churchill"). [Henry Petty-FitzMaurice, 5th Marquess of Lansdowne](/wiki/Henry_Petty-FitzMaurice,_5th_Marquess_of_Lansdowne "Henry Petty-FitzMaurice, 5th Marquess of Lansdowne") and [Victor Cavendish, 9th Duke of Devonshire](/wiki/Victor_Cavendish,_9th_Duke_of_Devonshire "Victor Cavendish, 9th Duke of Devonshire") both also served in the British Cabinet following their viceregal careers, and Lansdowne went on to serve as leader of the [Conservative Party](/wiki/Conservative_Party_(UK) "Conservative Party (UK)") in the House of Lords for over a decade.
